  2022-10: Strategy -2.1%, Benchmark -9.3% (+7.2%)
  2020-03: Strategy -8.5%, Benchmark -12.5% (+4.0%)

Worst Relative Months:
  2021-11: Strategy +1.2%, Benchmark +6.8% (-5.6%)
  2023-12: Strategy +2.1%, Benchmark +4.5% (-2.4%)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Attribution - Detailed return attribution
  • Metrics - All performance metrics
  • Walk-Forward - Validation methodology

docs.title: Portfolio Constraints
docs.text: Apply realistic constraints to portfolio weights.

docs.title: Why Constraints?
docs.text:
Unconstrained portfolios can have:

  • Extreme positions (50%+ in one stock)
  • Huge sector concentrations
  • Excessive leverage
  • Impractical trade sizes

Constraints make backtests realistic.

docs.title: Maximum Position Size
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio constrained:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

  constraints:
    max_position = 0.05  # No position > 5%

  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Minimum Position Size
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  min_position = 0.005  # No position < 0.5%

Small positions add costs without meaningful impact.

docs.title: Position Cap in long_short
docs.text:
Text Only
// Built-in position cap
weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2, cap=0.05)

docs.title: Gross Exposure
docs.text:
Total long + short exposure:

Text Only
constraints:
  max_gross = 2.0   # Max 200% gross (100% long + 100% short)
  min_gross = 1.5   # Min 150% gross

docs.title: Net Exposure
docs.text:
Long minus short exposure:

Text Only
constraints:
  max_net = 0.1    # Max 10% net long
  min_net = -0.1   # Max 10% net short

docs.title: Dollar Neutral
docs.text:
Enforce exact dollar neutrality:

Text Only
constraints:
  dollar_neutral = true  # Sum of weights = 0

docs.title: Maximum Sector Weight
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  max_sector = 0.25  # No sector > 25% of portfolio

docs.title: Sector Neutrality
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  sector_neutral = true  # Each sector sums to 0

docs.title: Relative Sector Bounds
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  sector_deviation = 0.05  # Within 5% of benchmark sector weights

docs.title: Maximum Turnover
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  max_turnover = 0.25  # Max 25% turnover per rebalance

docs.title: Minimum Trade Size
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  min_trade = 0.01  # Don't trade if change < 1%

This prevents excessive small trades.

docs.title: Maximum Leverage
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  max_leverage = 2.0  # Max 2x leverage

docs.title: Long/Short Limits
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  max_long = 1.0   # Max 100% long
  max_short = 1.0  # Max 100% short

docs.title: Minimum Volume
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  min_adv = 1000000  # Min $1M average daily volume

docs.title: Volume Participation
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  max_participation = 0.1  # Don't exceed 10% of daily volume

docs.title: Market Cap Filter
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  min_market_cap = 1000000000  # Min $1B market cap

docs.title: Tracking Error
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  max_tracking_error = 0.05  # Max 5% tracking error vs benchmark

docs.title: Beta
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  max_beta = 1.2
  min_beta = 0.8
  # Portfolio beta between 0.8 and 1.2

docs.title: Volatility Target
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  target_volatility = 0.15  # Target 15% annual vol

docs.title: Full Constraints Block
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio constrained:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

  constraints:
    // Position limits
    max_position = 0.05
    min_position = 0.005

    // Exposure limits
    max_gross = 2.0
    dollar_neutral = true

    // Sector limits
    max_sector = 0.25
    sector_neutral = true

    // Trading limits
    max_turnover = 0.25
    min_trade = 0.01

    // Liquidity
    min_adv = 1000000

  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Constraint Priority
docs.text:
When constraints conflict, sigc applies them in order:

  1. Liquidity - Filter out illiquid stocks
  2. Position limits - Cap individual positions
  3. Sector limits - Adjust sector exposures
  4. Exposure limits - Adjust gross/net exposure
  5. Turnover limits - Reduce trades if needed

docs.title: Hard Constraints (Strict)
docs.text:
Must be satisfied exactly:

Text Only
constraints:
  max_position = 0.05  # Hard limit

docs.title: Soft Constraints (Penalties)
docs.text:
Preferred but can be violated:

Text Only
constraints:
  soft:
    target_turnover = 0.15
    turnover_penalty = 0.01  # 1% penalty per 1% excess turnover

docs.title: Dynamic Constraints
docs.text:
Constraints that vary over time:

Text Only
signal vol_regime:
  vol = rolling_std(ret(prices, 1), 60)
  high_vol = vol > quantile(vol, 0.8)
  emit high_vol

portfolio adaptive:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

  constraints:
    // Reduce exposure in high volatility
    max_gross = where(vol_regime, 1.5, 2.0)

  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Constraint Violations Report
docs.text:
Check constraint adherence:

Bash
sigc run strategy.sig --report constraints

Output:

Text Only
Constraint Violations Report:
=============================

max_position (5%):
  Violations: 0
  Max observed: 4.8%

max_sector (25%):
  Violations: 3
  Dates: 2021-03-15 (Tech: 27%), 2021-04-12 (Tech: 26%), 2022-01-03 (Energy: 26%)

max_turnover (25%):
  Violations: 12
  Average excess: 3.2%
  Max excess: 8.5%

dollar_neutral:
  Max deviation: 0.2%
  Average deviation: 0.05%

docs.title: Conservative Institutional
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio institutional:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

  constraints:
    max_position = 0.03      # Max 3% per name
    max_sector = 0.20        # Max 20% per sector
    max_gross = 1.5          # Max 150% gross
    dollar_neutral = true    # Market neutral
    min_adv = 5000000        # Min $5M ADV
    max_turnover = 0.20      # Max 20% monthly turnover

  costs = tc.bps(10)
  backtest rebal=21 from 2015-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Aggressive Hedge Fund
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio hedge_fund:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.1, bottom=0.1)

  constraints:
    max_position = 0.10      # Max 10% per name
    max_gross = 3.0          # Max 300% gross
    max_net = 0.3            # Max 30% directional
    max_sector = 0.30        # Max 30% per sector

  costs = tc.bps(5) + slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.05)
  backtest rebal=5 from 2015-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Long-Only with Tracking
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio long_only:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.3, bottom=0)  # Long only

  constraints:
    min_position = 0.001     # Min 0.1% (avoid tiny positions)
    max_position = 0.05      # Max 5%
    max_gross = 1.0          # Fully invested (100%)
    sector_deviation = 0.05  # Within 5% of benchmark sectors
    max_tracking_error = 0.03  # Max 3% tracking error

  backtest rebal=21 benchmark=SPY from 2015-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: 1. Start Constrained
docs.text:
Text Only
// Better to be conservative
constraints:
  max_position = 0.03  # Not 0.10

docs.title: 2. Match Real-World Limits
docs.text:
Text Only
// If you trade a $100M portfolio:
constraints:
  max_participation = 0.05  # 5% of daily volume
  min_adv = 1000000         # $1M minimum

docs.title: 3. Include Turnover Limits
docs.text:
Text Only
constraints:
  max_turnover = 0.20  # Realistic for monthly rebal

docs.title: 4. Test Without Constraints First
docs.text: Compare constrained vs unconstrained to see impact.

docs.title: 5. Document Your Constraints
Next Steps
docs.text:
• Benchmark Analysis - Benchmark comparison
  • Cost Models - Transaction costs
  • Walk-Forward - Validation with constraints

docs.title: Cost Models
docs.text: Model transaction costs realistically for accurate backtests.

docs.title: Why Costs Matter
docs.text:
Transaction costs can significantly impact returns:

Annual Turnover Cost (10 bps) Cost (25 bps) 100% 0.1% 0.25% 250% 0.25% 0.625% 500% 0.5% 1.25% 1000% 1.0% 2.5%

A strategy with 15% gross returns and 500% turnover might only deliver 13.75% net returns.

docs.title: Fixed Basis Points
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.bps(5)  # 5 basis points per trade
  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

This applies 0.05% cost to each dollar traded.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Text Only
Trade: Sell 2% of AAPL, Buy 2% of MSFT
Total traded: 4% of portfolio
Cost: 4% × 0.05% = 0.002% of portfolio

docs.title: Per-Share Commission
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.per_share(0.005)  # $0.005 per share
  backtest ...

docs.title: Per-Trade Commission
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.per_trade(5.00)  # $5 per trade
  backtest ...

docs.title: Tiered Commission
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.tiered(
    tiers = [
      { volume = 1000000, rate = 0.0035 },
      { volume = 10000000, rate = 0.0020 },
      { default_rate = 0.0010 }
    ]
  )
  backtest ...

docs.title: Slippage Models
docs.text: Slippage captures market impact and execution quality.

docs.title: Fixed Slippage
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = slippage.bps(5)  # 5 bps slippage
  backtest ...

docs.title: Volume-Based Slippage
docs.text:
Cost increases with trade size relative to volume:

Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = slippage.volume_pct(coef=0.1)
  // Slippage = 0.1 × (trade_value / daily_volume)
  backtest ...

docs.title: Square-Root Model
docs.text:
Standard market impact model:

\[\text{Impact} = \sigma \cdot \text{coef} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\text{trade\_size}}{\text{ADV}}}\] Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.1)
  backtest ...

docs.title: Linear Model
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = slippage.model("linear", coef=0.05)
  backtest ...

docs.title: Combined Costs
docs.text:
Combine multiple cost components:

Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.bps(5) + slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.1)
  backtest ...

docs.title: Full Cost Model
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = (
    tc.bps(3) +                           # Bid-ask spread
    tc.per_share(0.001) +                 # Broker commission
    slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.05) +  # Market impact
    tc.bps(1)                             # Exchange fees
  )
  backtest ...

docs.title: Asymmetric Costs
docs.text:
Different costs for buying vs selling:

Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.asymmetric(
    buy = tc.bps(5),
    sell = tc.bps(7)  # Short-selling costs more
  )
  backtest ...

docs.title: Borrow Cost
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.bps(5) + borrow.rate(0.005)  # 0.5% annual borrow rate
  backtest ...

docs.title: Hard-to-Borrow
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.bps(5) + borrow.htb(
    default_rate = 0.005,
    htb_rate = 0.20,     # 20% for hard-to-borrow
    htb_list = "htb_stocks.csv"
  )
  backtest ...

docs.title: Institutional Trading
docs.text: Component Large Cap Small Cap Bid-Ask Spread 1-3 bps 5-15 bps Commission 1-2 bps 1-2 bps Market Impact 2-10 bps 10-50 bps Total 5-15 bps 20-70 bps

docs.title: Retail Trading
docs.text: Component Commission-Free Traditional Bid-Ask Spread 2-5 bps 2-5 bps Commission 0 bps 5-10 bps Payment for Order Flow 1-3 bps 0 bps Total 3-8 bps 7-15 bps

docs.title: Check Breakeven Turnover
docs.text:
Bash
sigc analyze strategy.sig --breakeven-turnover --cost-bps 10

Output:

Text Only
Gross Return: 15.2%
Cost per 100% Turnover: 0.2%
Breakeven Turnover: 7600%
Current Turnover: 250%
Net Return: 14.7%

docs.title: Turnover Reduction
docs.text:
Add turnover penalty:

Text Only
portfolio low_turnover:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

  // Don't trade small changes
  constraints:
    min_trade = 0.01  # Don't trade if change < 1%

  backtest ...

docs.title: Rebalancing Frequency
docs.text:
Less frequent rebalancing reduces costs:

Text Only
// High turnover
portfolio daily:
  backtest rebal=1 ...  // High costs

// Lower turnover
portfolio monthly:
  backtest rebal=21 ...  // Lower costs

docs.title: Sensitivity Analysis
docs.text:
Test different cost assumptions:

Text Only
portfolio low_cost:
  costs = tc.bps(5)
  backtest ...

portfolio mid_cost:
  costs = tc.bps(15)
  backtest ...

portfolio high_cost:
  costs = tc.bps(30)
  backtest ...

docs.title: CLI Analysis
docs.text:
Bash
sigc run strategy.sig --cost-sensitivity 5,10,15,20,25,30

Output:

Text Only
Cost Sensitivity Analysis:
Cost (bps) | Net Return | Sharpe | Breakeven
-----------+------------+--------+----------
5          | 14.7%      | 0.95   | Yes
10         | 14.2%      | 0.92   | Yes
15         | 13.7%      | 0.89   | Yes
20         | 13.2%      | 0.86   | Yes
25         | 12.7%      | 0.82   | Yes
30         | 12.2%      | 0.79   | Yes

docs.title: 1. Be Conservative
docs.text:
Text Only
// Assume higher costs than expected
costs = tc.bps(15)  // Not tc.bps(5)

docs.title: 2. Include All Components
docs.text:
Text Only
// Don't just use commission
costs = (
  tc.bps(3) +           // Spread
  tc.per_share(0.001) + // Commission
  slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.1) +  // Impact
  borrow.rate(0.005)    // Short costs
)

docs.title: 3. Test Sensitivity
docs.text:
Text Only
// Strategy should work across reasonable cost assumptions
portfolio conservative:
  costs = tc.bps(20)  // Double expected costs
  backtest ...

docs.title: 4. Consider Capacity
docs.text:
Large positions have higher impact:

Text Only
// Add position cap to manage impact
weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2, cap=0.03)

docs.title: 5. Account for Liquidity
docs.text:
Text Only
// Higher costs for illiquid stocks
costs = slippage.model("square-root",
  coef=0.1,
  volume_threshold=1000000  // Higher impact for low-volume stocks
)

docs.title: Example: Full Cost Model
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "prices.parquet"
  format = parquet
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices
    volume: Numeric

signal momentum:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, 60))

portfolio realistic:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2, cap=0.03)

  // Realistic institutional cost model
  costs = (
    tc.bps(2) +                              // Half spread
    tc.per_share(0.001) +                    // Commission
    slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.1) +  // Market impact
    borrow.rate(0.003)                       // Borrow cost
  )

  // Turnover control
  constraints:
    min_trade = 0.005  // Don't trade < 0.5% changes

  backtest rebal=21 from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Metrics - Understanding return metrics
  • Walk-Forward - Out-of-sample testing
  • Constraints - Portfolio constraints

docs.title: Performance Metrics
docs.text: Comprehensive metrics for evaluating strategy performance.

docs.title: Total Return
docs.text:
Cumulative return over the backtest period:

\[\text{Total Return} = \frac{V_{end} - V_{start}}{V_{start}}\] Text Only
Total Return: 85.2%

docs.title: CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate)
docs.text:
Annualized return accounting for compounding:

\[\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{V_{end}}{V_{start}}\right)^{\frac{1}{years}} - 1\] Text Only
CAGR: 13.1%

docs.title: Rolling Returns
docs.text:
Returns over rolling windows:

Period 1M 3M 6M 1Y 3Y Return 2.8% 7.2% 12.1% 18.5% 52.3%

docs.title: Volatility
docs.text:
Annualized standard deviation of returns:

\[\text{Volatility} = \sigma_{daily} \times \sqrt{252}\] Text Only
Annualized Volatility: 15.2%

docs.title: Downside Volatility
docs.text:
Standard deviation of negative returns only:

\[\text{Downside Vol} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{r_i < 0} r_i^2}{n}} \times \sqrt{252}\] Text Only
Downside Volatility: 10.8%

docs.title: Maximum Drawdown
docs.text:
Largest peak-to-trough decline:

\[\text{MDD} = \max_t \left(\frac{\max_{s \leq t} V_s - V_t}{\max_{s \leq t} V_s}\right)\] Text Only
Max Drawdown: -18.5%
Max Drawdown Duration: 145 days
Max Drawdown Start: 2022-01-03
Max Drawdown End: 2022-10-12
Recovery Date: 2023-02-15

docs.title: Value at Risk (VaR)
docs.text:
Maximum expected loss at confidence level:

Text Only
VaR (95%): -2.1% (daily)
           -4.5% (weekly)
           -9.2% (monthly)

VaR (99%): -3.5% (daily)

Interpretation: 95% of days, losses won't exceed 2.1%.

docs.title: Conditional VaR (CVaR / Expected Shortfall)
docs.text:
Average loss beyond VaR:

Text Only
CVaR (95%): -3.2% (daily)

Interpretation: When losses exceed VaR, average loss is 3.2%.

docs.title: Sharpe Ratio
docs.text:
Return per unit of risk:

\[\text{Sharpe} = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}\] Text Only
Sharpe Ratio: 0.86

Interpretation:

Sharpe Quality < 0.5 Poor 0.5-1.0 Average 1.0-1.5 Good > 1.5 Excellent

docs.title: Sortino Ratio
docs.text:
Return per unit of downside risk:

\[\text{Sortino} = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_{downside}}\] Text Only
Sortino Ratio: 1.21

docs.title: Calmar Ratio
docs.text:
CAGR relative to maximum drawdown:

\[\text{Calmar} = \frac{\text{CAGR}}{|\text{Max Drawdown}|}\] Text Only
Calmar Ratio: 0.71

docs.title: Information Ratio
docs.text:
Excess return per unit of tracking error:

\[\text{IR} = \frac{R_p - R_b}{\text{TE}}\] Text Only
Information Ratio: 0.45

docs.title: Omega Ratio
docs.text:
Probability-weighted gains vs losses:

\[\Omega = \frac{\int_\theta^\infty (1 - F(r)) dr}{\int_{-\infty}^\theta F(r) dr}\] Text Only
Omega Ratio (0%): 1.35

docs.title: Alpha and Beta
docs.text:
CAPM regression coefficients:

\[R_p - R_f = \alpha + \beta (R_m - R_f) + \epsilon\] Text Only
Alpha: 4.2% (annualized)
Beta: 0.72

docs.title: Tracking Error
docs.text:
Volatility of excess returns:

Text Only
Tracking Error: 9.3%

docs.title: Up/Down Capture
docs.text:
Performance in up vs down markets:

Text Only
Up Capture: 85%    (captures 85% of benchmark gains)
Down Capture: 62%  (captures 62% of benchmark losses)
Capture Ratio: 1.37  (85/62)

docs.title: Correlation
docs.text:
Text Only
Correlation with SPY: 0.78
Correlation with QQQ: 0.65

docs.title: Skewness
docs.text:
Asymmetry of returns:

Text Only
Skewness: -0.15
  • Negative: More extreme losses than gains
  • Positive: More extreme gains than losses

docs.title: Kurtosis
docs.text:
Tail heaviness:

Text Only
Kurtosis: 3.8 (excess: 0.8)
  • 3.0: Normal distribution
  • >3.0: Fat tails (more extreme events)
  • <3.0: Thin tails

docs.title: Win Rate
docs.text:
Percentage of positive return periods:

Text Only
Win Rate (daily): 52.3%
Win Rate (weekly): 55.8%
Win Rate (monthly): 58.3%

docs.title: Profit Factor
docs.text:
Gross profits / Gross losses:

Text Only
Profit Factor: 1.28

docs.title: Turnover
docs.text:
Portfolio turnover rate:

Text Only
Annual Turnover: 245%
Monthly Turnover: 20.4%
Per-Rebalance Turnover: 18.5%

docs.title: Trade Count
docs.text:
Text Only
Total Trades: 4,523
Avg Trades per Rebalance: 85

docs.title: Position Count
docs.text:
Text Only
Avg Long Positions: 42
Avg Short Positions: 38
Max Positions: 95

docs.title: Concentration
docs.text:
Text Only
Top 10 Positions: 35% of portfolio
HHI (Herfindahl): 0.02
Effective N: 48 (1/HHI)

docs.title: Python Integration
docs.text:
Python
import pysigc

results = pysigc.run("strategy.sig")
print(f"Sharpe: {results.sharpe_ratio:.2f}")

docs.title: What Makes a Good Strategy?
docs.text: Metric Poor Acceptable Good Excellent Sharpe <0.5 0.5-0.8 0.8-1.2 >1.2 Max DD >30% 20-30% 10-20% <10% Calmar <0.5 0.5-0.8 0.8-1.2 >1.2 Win Rate <50% 50-55% 55-60% >60%

docs.title: Red Flags
docs.text:
• Sharpe > 2.5 without explanation (likely overfit)
  • Max DD recovering immediately (data snooping)
  • No down years in 5+ year backtest
  • Turnover > 1000% annually (cost-prohibitive)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Cost Models - Impact of trading costs
  • Benchmark Analysis - Detailed benchmark comparison
  • Walk-Forward - Out-of-sample validation

docs.title: Walk-Forward Testing
docs.text: Validate strategies with proper out-of-sample testing.

docs.title: The Problem with Simple Backtesting
docs.text:
Simple backtests can overfit:

Text Only
1. Test many parameter combinations
2. Pick the "best" one
3. Deploy → Poor real-world performance

This is data snooping - using future information to select parameters.

docs.title: Basic Walk-Forward
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

  backtest walk_forward(
    train_years = 5,
    test_years = 2,
    step_years = 2
  ) from 2010-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description train_years Length of training window test_years Length of test window step_years How far to move between windows

docs.title: Parameter Optimization
docs.text:
Optimize parameters in each training window:

Text Only
params:
  lookback: range(20, 120, 20)  # 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120
  top_pct: range(0.1, 0.4, 0.1)  # 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4

signal momentum:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, lookback))

portfolio optimized:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=top_pct, bottom=top_pct)

  backtest walk_forward(
    train_years = 5,
    test_years = 2,
    step_years = 2,
    optimize = maximize("sharpe")  # Optimize for Sharpe in train
  ) from 2010-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Optimization Objectives
docs.text: Objective Description sharpe Maximize Sharpe ratio return Maximize total return calmar Maximize Calmar ratio sortino Maximize Sortino ratio min_drawdown Minimize max drawdown

docs.title: Walk-Forward Output
docs.text:
Text Only
Walk-Forward Results:
====================

Window 1 (Train: 2010-2014, Test: 2015-2016):
  Optimal Params: lookback=60, top_pct=0.2
  Train Sharpe: 1.25
  Test Sharpe: 0.92

Window 2 (Train: 2012-2016, Test: 2017-2018):
  Optimal Params: lookback=60, top_pct=0.2
  Train Sharpe: 1.18
  Test Sharpe: 0.85

Window 3 (Train: 2014-2018, Test: 2019-2020):
  Optimal Params: lookback=80, top_pct=0.2
  Train Sharpe: 1.32
  Test Sharpe: 0.78

Window 4 (Train: 2016-2020, Test: 2021-2022):
  Optimal Params: lookback=60, top_pct=0.3
  Train Sharpe: 1.15
  Test Sharpe: 0.65

Combined Out-of-Sample (2015-2022):
  Sharpe Ratio: 0.80
  CAGR: 11.2%
  Max Drawdown: -22.5%

Degradation: 35% (avg train Sharpe vs avg test Sharpe)

docs.title: Rolling Walk-Forward
docs.text:
Fixed-length training window that rolls forward:

Text Only
portfolio rolling:
  backtest walk_forward(
    train_years = 3,
    test_years = 1,
    step_years = 1,
    rolling = true
  ) from 2010-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Cross-Validation
docs.text:
K-fold cross-validation for robustness:

Text Only
portfolio cross_val:
  backtest cross_validate(
    folds = 5,
    purge_days = 21  # Gap between train and test to prevent leakage
  ) from 2010-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Purged Cross-Validation
docs.text:
The purge_days parameter prevents data leakage from autocorrelation:

Text Only
[=== Train ===][purge][=== Test ===][purge][=== Train ===]
                 ↑                     ↑
              21-day gap           21-day gap

docs.title: Combinatorial Purged Cross-Validation
docs.text:
More sophisticated validation for overlapping data:

Text Only
portfolio cpcv:
  backtest cpcv(
    n_splits = 5,
    n_test_groups = 2,
    purge_days = 21,
    embargo_days = 5
  ) from 2010-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Degradation Ratio
docs.text:
Ratio of out-of-sample to in-sample performance:

Text Only
Degradation = (In-Sample Sharpe - Out-of-Sample Sharpe) / In-Sample Sharpe
Degradation Interpretation < 20% Good - strategy likely robust 20-40% Acceptable - some overfitting 40-60% Concerning - significant overfitting > 60% Poor - strategy likely overfit

docs.title: Parameter Stability
docs.text:
Check if optimal parameters are stable:

Text Only
Window 1: lookback=60, top=0.2
Window 2: lookback=60, top=0.2
Window 3: lookback=80, top=0.2  <- Small variation
Window 4: lookback=60, top=0.3  <- Some variation

Conclusion: Reasonably stable, lookback=60 is robust

Unstable parameters indicate overfitting:

Text Only
Window 1: lookback=20, top=0.1
Window 2: lookback=120, top=0.4  <- Huge change!
Window 3: lookback=40, top=0.2
Window 4: lookback=100, top=0.3

Conclusion: Parameters not robust, likely overfit

docs.title: 1. Use Sufficient History
docs.text:
Text Only
// At least 5 years of training data
backtest walk_forward(
  train_years = 5,  # Not 1 or 2 years
  ...
)

docs.title: 2. Test Multiple Windows
docs.text:
Text Only
// At least 3-4 out-of-sample windows
backtest walk_forward(
  train_years = 5,
  test_years = 2,
  step_years = 2
) from 2005-01-01 to 2024-12-31
// Creates 5+ windows

docs.title: 3. Don't Over-Optimize
4. Include Transaction Costs
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  costs = tc.bps(10)  # Include in walk-forward
  backtest walk_forward(...) from ...

docs.title: 5. Use Purging for Financial Data
docs.text:
Text Only
backtest cross_validate(
  folds = 5,
  purge_days = 21  # Important for return autocorrelation
)

docs.title: Example: Complete Walk-Forward Analysis
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "prices.parquet"
  format = parquet

params:
  lookback: [40, 60, 80]
  top_pct: [0.15, 0.20, 0.25]

signal momentum:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, lookback))

portfolio walk_forward_test:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=top_pct, bottom=top_pct, cap=0.03)

  costs = tc.bps(10)

  backtest walk_forward(
    train_years = 5,
    test_years = 2,
    step_years = 2,
    optimize = maximize("sharpe"),
    min_sharpe = 0.5  # Require min in-sample Sharpe
  ) from 2005-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Run Analysis
docs.text:
Bash
sigc run strategy.sig --walk-forward --report detailed

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Text Only

Results by Window:
                    In-Sample              Out-of-Sample
Window | Period     | Params     | Sharpe | Period     | Sharpe
-------+------------+------------+--------+------------+-------

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Understanding sigc's core concepts will help you build better trading strategies.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
sigc is built around four fundamental concepts:

  1. Signals - Numerical scores that rank assets
  2. Portfolios - Weight allocations based on signals
  3. Backtests - Historical simulations of portfolio performance
  4. Type System - Compile-time checking of operations
graph LR
    A[Data] --> B[Signal]
    B --> C[Portfolio]
    C --> D[Backtest]
    D --> E[Metrics]

docs.title: Quick Concept Map
docs.text: Concept What It Is Example Signal Score per asset Momentum, value, quality Portfolio Weight allocation Long/short, long-only Backtest Historical simulation 2020-2024 test Type System Safety checking Shape, dtype inference

docs.title: 1. Load Data
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"

Data flows into the system as time-series matrices (dates × assets).

docs.title: 2. Define Signals
docs.text:
Text Only
signal momentum:
  returns = ret(prices, 20)
  emit zscore(returns)

Signals transform data into scores that rank assets at each point in time.

docs.title: 3. Construct Portfolios
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

Portfolio construction converts scores into tradeable weights.

docs.title: 4. Run Backtests
docs.text:
Text Only
  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

Backtesting simulates the strategy over historical data.

docs.title: 5. Analyze Results
docs.text:
Text Only
=== Backtest Results ===
Sharpe Ratio:    1.45
Max Drawdown:    8.12%

Metrics quantify strategy performance.

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
sigc guarantees deterministic results:

  • Same inputs → same outputs, always
  • Content-addressed caching with blake3 hashes
  • No hidden randomness or state

docs.title: Type Safety
docs.text:
The type system catches errors at compile time:

Text Only
signal bad:
  x = ret(prices, 20)      // Returns: time-series
  y = zscore(x)            // Error: zscore expects cross-sectional

Learn more in Type System.

docs.title: Composability
docs.text:
Build complex strategies from simple components:

Text Only
fn vol_adj(x, lookback):
  x / rolling_std(ret(x, 1), lookback)

macro momentum_signal(px: expr, lookback: number = 20):
  let r = ret(px, lookback)
  emit zscore(vol_adj(r, 60))

signal combo:
  mom = momentum_signal(prices)
  rev = -zscore(ret(prices, 5))
  emit 0.7 * mom + 0.3 * rev

docs.title: Section Overview
docs.text: Page Description Signals What signals are and how to build them Portfolio Construction Converting signals to weights Backtesting Simulation methodology Type System Shape-aware type checking Architecture System components and design

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Start with What Are Signals? to understand the foundation of sigc strategies.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This page describes sigc's system architecture for developers who want to understand how it works under the hood.

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text:
sigc is a single binary that combines multiple components:

graph TB
    subgraph "sigc Binary"
        CLI[CLI Interface]
        Compiler[sig_compiler]
        Runtime[sig_runtime]
        Cache[sig_cache]
        LSP[sig_lsp]
    end

    CLI --> Compiler
    Compiler --> Cache
    Compiler --> Runtime
    Runtime --> Cache
    LSP --> Compiler

    subgraph "External"
        VSCode[VS Code]
        Data[(Data Sources)]
        DB[(PostgreSQL)]
    end

    VSCode --> LSP
    Runtime --> Data
    Runtime --> DB

docs.title: 1. CLI (sigc)
docs.text:
The main entry point. Provides subcommands:

Command Description compile Parse and type-check .sig file run Compile and execute backtest explain Show IR structure diff Compare strategies cache Manage cache daemon Start RPC server request Send commands to daemon

Location: crates/sigc/src/main.rs

docs.title: 2. Compiler (sig_compiler)
docs.text:
Parses DSL and produces typed IR:

graph LR
    A[Source .sig] --> B[Parser]
    B --> C[AST]
    C --> D[Type Checker]
    D --> E[IR Lowerer]
    E --> F[Optimized IR]

Stages:

  1. Parsing: Chumsky-based parser → AST
  2. Type Checking: Infer types, check constraints
  3. IR Lowering: AST → Intermediate Representation
  4. Optimization: Constant folding, dead code elimination

Location: crates/sig_compiler/src/

docs.title: 3. Runtime (sig_runtime)
docs.text:
Executes IR and runs backtests:

Components:

Module Purpose engine.rs Evaluates IR nodes backtest.rs Simulation loop kernels.rs Rolling statistics simd_kernels.rs SIMD-optimized ops costs.rs Transaction costs portfolio_opt.rs Optimization safety.rs Circuit breakers

Location: crates/sig_runtime/src/

docs.title: 4. Types (sig_types)
docs.text:
Shared type definitions:

  • DType: Data types (Float64, Int32, Bool, etc.)
  • Shape: Tensor dimensions
  • Operator: 120+ operators
  • Ir: Intermediate representation
  • TypeAnnotation: Combined dtype + shape

Location: crates/sig_types/src/lib.rs

docs.title: 5. Cache (sig_cache)
docs.text:
Content-addressed caching:

graph LR
    A[Source Code] --> B[blake3 Hash]
    B --> C{In Cache?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Load IR]
    C -->|No| E[Compile]
    E --> F[Store IR]
    F --> D
  • Backend: sled embedded database
  • Hashing: blake3 for speed
  • Contents: Compiled IR, computed artifacts

Location: crates/sig_cache/src/lib.rs

docs.title: 6. Language Server (sig_lsp)
docs.text:
LSP implementation for IDE support:

  • Real-time diagnostics
  • Hover documentation
  • Code completion
  • Go-to-definition

Location: crates/sig_lsp/src/main.rs

docs.title: 7. Python Bindings (pysigc)
docs.text:
PyO3 bindings for notebooks:

Python
import pysigc
result = pysigc.backtest(source)

Location: crates/pysigc/src/lib.rs

docs.title: Compilation Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant CLI
    participant Cache
    participant Compiler

    User->>CLI: sigc compile strategy.sig
    CLI->>Cache: Check hash
    Cache-->>CLI: Cache miss
    CLI->>Compiler: Parse source
    Compiler->>Compiler: Type check
    Compiler->>Compiler: Lower to IR
    Compiler-->>CLI: Compiled IR
    CLI->>Cache: Store IR
    CLI-->>User: Success

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant CLI
    participant Runtime
    participant Data

    User->>CLI: sigc run strategy.sig
    CLI->>Runtime: Execute IR
    Runtime->>Data: Load prices.csv
    Data-->>Runtime: DataFrame
    Runtime->>Runtime: Evaluate operators
    Runtime->>Runtime: Construct portfolio
    Runtime->>Runtime: Run backtest loop
    Runtime-->>CLI: BacktestReport
    CLI-->>User: Display metrics

docs.title: Daemon Architecture
docs.text:
For long-lived services, sigc supports daemon mode:

graph TB
    subgraph "Daemon Process"
        Listener[NNG Listener]
        Workers[Worker Pool]
        SharedCache[Shared Cache]
    end

    subgraph "Clients"
        CLI1[sigc request]
        CLI2[sigc request]
        Python[pysigc]
    end

    CLI1 -->|REQ/REP| Listener
    CLI2 -->|REQ/REP| Listener
    Python -->|REQ/REP| Listener

    Listener --> Workers
    Workers --> SharedCache

Protocol: NNG (nanomsg-next-gen) REQ/REP

Commands:

Bash
# Start daemon
sigc daemon --listen tcp://127.0.0.1:7240 --workers 8

# Send requests
sigc request ping
sigc request compile strategy.sig
sigc request run strategy.sig
sigc request status
sigc request shutdown

docs.title: SIMD Optimization
docs.text:
Rolling statistics use SIMD instructions:

Rust
// SIMD-optimized rolling mean
pub fn rolling_mean_simd(data: &[f64], window: usize) -> Vec<f64> {
    // Uses AVX2/NEON for parallel computation
    ...
}

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Default SIGC_CACHE_DIR Cache location ~/.cache/sigc SIGC_LOG_LEVEL Log verbosity info AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID S3 access - AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY S3 secret -

docs.title: TOML Configuration
docs.text:
TOML
# sigc.toml
[cache]
path = "/data/sigc_cache"

[database]
host = "localhost"
port = 5432
database = "sigc"

[execution]
workers = 8
simd = true

[backtest]
default_cost_bps = 5

docs.title: Compiler Errors
docs.text:
Reported with source locations:

Text Only
Error: Unknown function 'zsocre'
  --> strategy.sig:5:7
    |
  5 |   z = zsocre(returns)
    |       ^^^^^^
    |
help: Did you mean 'zscore'?

Implementation: ariadne crate for pretty-printing

docs.title: Runtime Errors
docs.text:
Wrapped in Result types:

Rust
pub fn execute(&mut self, ir: &Ir) -> Result<BacktestReport, RuntimeError> {
    ...
}

docs.title: Test Structure
docs.text:
Text Only
crates/sigc/tests/
├── integration.rs   # End-to-end tests
├── operators.rs     # Operator tests
└── strategies.rs    # Strategy parsing tests

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
Bash
# All tests
cargo test

# Specific crate
cargo test -p sig_runtime

# With output
cargo test -- --nocapture

Coverage: 330+ tests across all crates

docs.title: Memory Efficiency
docs.text:
• Columnar storage reduces memory bandwidth
  • Memory-mapped files for large datasets
  • Lazy evaluation where possible

docs.title: Parallelization
docs.text:
• Rayon for data-parallel operations
  • SIMD for rolling computations
  • Async I/O for database operations

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
• Content-addressed caching avoids recomputation
  • IR and intermediate results cached
  • Cache invalidation via blake3 hashes

docs.title: Adding New Operators
docs.text:
1. Define operator in sig_types:
Rust
// In crates/sig_types/src/lib.rs
pub enum Operator {
    // ...existing...
    MyNewOp,
}
  1. Implement in runtime:
Rust
// In crates/sig_runtime/src/engine.rs
fn eval_my_new_op(&self, inputs: &[Value]) -> Result<Value> {
    // Implementation
}
  1. Add parser support:
Rust
// In crates/sig_compiler/src/parser.rs
"my_new_op" => Operator::MyNewOp
  1. Add tests:
Rust
// In crates/sigc/tests/operators.rs
#[test]
fn test_my_new_op() {
    // Test implementation
}

docs.title: Adding New Data Sources
docs.text:
Implement the Connector trait:

Rust
pub trait Connector: Send + Sync {
    fn load(&self, query: &str) -> Result<DataFrame>;
    fn is_available(&self) -> bool;
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• CLI Reference - Command details
  • Rust API - Programmatic usage
  • Contributing - Development setup

docs.title: Backtesting Fundamentals
docs.text: Backtesting simulates how a strategy would have performed historically. This page covers the methodology and key concepts.

docs.title: What Is Backtesting?
docs.text:
Backtesting answers: "If I had traded this strategy historically, what would the results have been?"

Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31
Text Only
=== Backtest Results ===
Total Return:         45.23%
Annualized Return:    10.12%
Sharpe Ratio:          1.23
Max Drawdown:         12.45%
Turnover:            285.00%

docs.title: Daily Simulation Loop
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Start of Day t] --> B[Compute Signal]
    B --> C[Calculate Weights]
    C --> D[Execute Trades]
    D --> E[Apply Costs]
    E --> F[Record Returns]
    F --> G[Next Day t+1]
    G --> B

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
For each trading day:

  1. Signal Calculation: Compute signal using data through day t-1
  2. Weight Determination: Convert signals to target weights
  3. Trade Execution: Rebalance portfolio to target weights
  4. Cost Application: Deduct transaction costs
  5. Return Calculation: Compute day's P&L

docs.title: Look-Ahead Bias
docs.text:
sigc prevents look-ahead bias by ensuring signals use only past data:

Text Only
signal momentum:
  // ret(prices, 20) on day t uses prices[t-20:t-1]
  // NOT prices[t] (today's price)
  returns = ret(prices, 20)
  emit zscore(returns)

Avoid Look-Ahead Bias

Look-ahead bias occurs when a signal uses future information. This makes backtests unrealistically good. sigc's design prevents this, but be careful with external data.

docs.title: Return Metrics
docs.text: Metric Formula Interpretation Total Return \((P_{end} - P_{start}) / P_{start}\) Cumulative gain/loss Annualized Return \((1 + R_{total})^{252/days} - 1\) Yearly equivalent CAGR Same as annualized Compound annual growth

docs.title: Risk-Adjusted Metrics
docs.text: Metric Formula Good Value Sharpe Ratio \(\mu / \sigma\) (annualized) > 1.0 Sortino Ratio \(\mu / \sigma_{down}\) > 1.5 Calmar Ratio Return / Max Drawdown > 1.0

docs.title: Drawdown Metrics
docs.text: Metric Description Good Value Max Drawdown Largest peak-to-trough decline < 15% Avg Drawdown Mean of all drawdowns < 5% Drawdown Duration Time to recover from max DD < 1 year

docs.title: Trading Metrics
docs.text: Metric Description Typical Value Turnover Annual portfolio churn 200-500% Win Rate % of positive days 50-55% Profit Factor Avg win / Avg loss > 1.5

docs.title: Good Backtest
docs.text:
Text Only
Total Return:         85.00%
Annualized Return:    17.50%
Sharpe Ratio:          1.85
Max Drawdown:          9.20%
Turnover:            220.00%
  • High Sharpe (> 1.5)
  • Reasonable drawdown (< 15%)
  • Moderate turnover

docs.title: Suspicious Backtest
docs.text:
Text Only
Total Return:        450.00%
Annualized Return:    95.00%
Sharpe Ratio:          5.20
Max Drawdown:          2.10%
Turnover:            800.00%
  • Sharpe > 3 is rare in practice
  • Very low drawdown is suspicious
  • High turnover may indicate overfitting

docs.title: Warning Signs
docs.text:
1. Sharpe > 3: Likely overfitting or look-ahead bias
  2. No drawdowns: Strategy may be unrealistic
  3. Turnover > 1000%: Excessive trading
  4. Only recent performance: May not generalize

docs.title: Overfitting
docs.text:
Fitting too closely to historical data:

Text Only
// Bad: Too many tuned parameters
params:
  lookback = 17      // Why 17?
  skip = 3           // Why 3?
  winsor_pct = 0.023 // Suspiciously precise

Solutions:

  • Use simple, well-motivated parameters
  • Test out-of-sample
  • Use walk-forward optimization

docs.title: Survivorship Bias
docs.text:
Only testing stocks that exist today:

Problem: A 2010 backtest that excludes Lehman Brothers, Enron, etc.

Solutions:

  • Include delisted securities
  • Use point-in-time constituents
  • Be skeptical of perfect data

docs.title: Transaction Cost Underestimation
docs.text:
Text Only
// Too optimistic
costs = tc.bps(1)

// More realistic for equities
costs = tc.bps(5) + slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.1)

docs.title: Data Snooping
docs.text:
Testing many strategies and reporting the best:

Problem: If you test 100 strategies, some will look good by chance.

Solutions:

  • Pre-specify hypotheses
  • Adjust for multiple testing
  • Validate on holdout data

docs.title: 1. Split Data
docs.text:
Text Only
Full Period: 2010-2024
├── In-Sample (Training): 2010-2019
└── Out-of-Sample (Testing): 2020-2024

Develop strategy on training data, validate on test data.

docs.title: 2. Walk-Forward Testing
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Train 2010-2014] --> B[Test 2015]
    C[Train 2011-2015] --> D[Test 2016]
    E[Train 2012-2016] --> F[Test 2017]

See Walk-Forward Optimization.

docs.title: 3. Realistic Cost Modeling
docs.text:
Include:

  • Commission: 1-5 bps
  • Spread: 1-10 bps
  • Market impact: depends on size
  • Borrowing costs: for shorts

docs.title: 4. Multiple Time Periods
docs.text:
Test across different market regimes:

  • Bull markets (2010-2019)
  • Bear markets (2008-2009, 2020, 2022)
  • High volatility (2008, 2020)
  • Low volatility (2017)

docs.title: 5. Sensitivity Analysis
docs.text:
Test robustness to parameter changes:

Bash
# Test different lookbacks
sigc run strategy_10d.sig
sigc run strategy_20d.sig
sigc run strategy_40d.sig

# Compare results
sigc diff strategy_10d.sig strategy_20d.sig

docs.title: Date Range
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Rebalancing Frequency
docs.text:
Text Only
// Daily (default)
backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

// Weekly
backtest rebal=5 from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

// Monthly
backtest rebal=21 from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Benchmark
docs.text:
Text Only
backtest benchmark=SPY from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

Enables alpha, beta, and tracking error calculations.

docs.title: Transaction Costs
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = ...
  costs = tc.bps(5) + slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.1)
  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Export to JSON
docs.text:
Bash
sigc run strategy.sig --output results.json

docs.title: Compare Strategies
docs.text:
Bash
sigc diff strategy_a.sig strategy_b.sig

docs.title: View IR
docs.text:
Bash
sigc explain strategy.sig

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Performance Metrics - Detailed metrics
  • Transaction Costs - Cost modeling
  • Walk-Forward - Out-of-sample testing
  • Type System - How sigc ensures correctness

docs.title: Portfolio Construction
docs.text: Portfolio construction converts signals into tradeable portfolio weights.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The portfolio construction process:

graph LR
    A[Signal Scores] --> B[Ranking]
    B --> C[Weight Assignment]
    C --> D[Constraints]
    D --> E[Final Weights]

docs.title: Long-Short Portfolio
docs.text:
The most common approach: long winners, short losers.

Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2024-01-01 to 2024-12-31

This creates:

  • Long: Top 20% of assets (positive weights)
  • Short: Bottom 20% of assets (negative weights)
  • Neutral: Middle 60% (zero weight)

docs.title: Weight Distribution
docs.text:
With long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2) and 100 assets:

Text Only
Position    Count   Weight Each   Total
Long        20      +5%           +100%
Neutral     60      0%            0%
Short       20      -5%           -100%
------------------------------------------
Net Exposure                      0%
Gross Exposure                    200%

docs.title: Ranking
docs.text:
The rank() function converts raw scores to uniform ranks:

Text Only
signal example:
  raw_scores = zscore(ret(prices, 20))
  emit raw_scores

portfolio main:
  // rank() converts scores to 0-1 range
  weights = rank(example).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

docs.title: Rank vs Raw Scores
docs.text: Approach Pros Cons rank(signal) Robust to outliers Ignores signal magnitude signal directly Uses signal strength Sensitive to outliers

docs.title: Equal-Weighted Long-Short
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio equal_weight:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

Each long position: +1 / (N * top_pct) Each short position: -1 / (N * bottom_pct)

docs.title: Signal-Weighted
docs.text:
Weight proportional to signal strength:

Text Only
signal momentum:
  scores = zscore(ret(prices, 20))
  cleaned = winsor(scores, p=0.01)
  emit cleaned

portfolio signal_weight:
  // Scale signal to sum to desired gross exposure
  long_mask = momentum > 0
  short_mask = momentum < 0
  scaled = scale(abs(momentum))
  weights = where(long_mask, scaled, -scaled)

docs.title: Position Caps
docs.text:
Limit individual position sizes:

Text Only
portfolio capped:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2, cap=0.05)

With cap=0.05, no single position exceeds 5% of portfolio.

docs.title: Dollar-Neutral (Long-Short)
docs.text:
Net exposure = 0

Text Only
portfolio dollar_neutral:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  // Sum of longs = Sum of shorts (in absolute terms)

docs.title: Long-Only
docs.text:
No short positions:

Text Only
portfolio long_only:
  // Only go long top performers
  scores = rank(signal)
  top_mask = scores > 0.8  // Top 20%
  weights = where(top_mask, scale(scores), 0)

docs.title: Beta-Neutral
docs.text:
Net beta = 0

Text Only
portfolio beta_neutral:
  raw = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  // Adjust weights to achieve beta=0
  // (requires beta estimates)

docs.title: Sector-Neutral
docs.text:
Equal sector exposure to benchmark:

Text Only
signal sector_neutral:
  raw = zscore(ret(prices, 20))
  neutral = neutralize(raw, by=sector)
  emit neutral

portfolio sector_balanced:
  weights = rank(sector_neutral).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

docs.title: Position Limits
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio constrained:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(
    top=0.2,
    bottom=0.2,
    cap=0.05  // Max 5% per position
  )

docs.title: Turnover Limits
docs.text:
Limit daily trading (in Rust API):

Rust
let constraints = ConstraintSet::new()
    .with_turnover(0.25);  // Max 25% one-way turnover

docs.title: Sector Limits
docs.text:
Via Rust API:

Rust
let constraints = ConstraintSet::new()
    .add_sector(SectorConstraint {
        sector: "Technology".to_string(),
        max_weight: Some(0.30),  // Max 30% in tech
    });

docs.title: Daily Rebalancing
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio daily:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2024-01-01 to 2024-12-31
  // Rebalances every day by default

docs.title: Periodic Rebalancing
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio weekly:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest rebal=5 from 2024-01-01 to 2024-12-31
  // Rebalances every 5 days

docs.title: Monthly Rebalancing
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio monthly:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest rebal=21 from 2024-01-01 to 2024-12-31
  // Rebalances every 21 days (approx monthly)

docs.title: Basic Cost Model
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio with_costs:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  costs = tc.bps(5)  // 5 basis points
  backtest from 2024-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Advanced Cost Model
docs.text:
Text Only
portfolio institutional:
  weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  costs = tc.bps(5) + slippage.model("square-root", coef=0.1)
  backtest from 2024-01-01 to 2024-12-31

See Transaction Costs for details.

docs.title: Signal Combination (Before Ranking)
docs.text:
Text Only
signal combo:
  mom = zscore(ret(prices, 60))
  rev = -zscore(ret(prices, 5))
  emit 0.6 * mom + 0.4 * rev

portfolio combined:
  weights = rank(combo).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)

docs.title: Portfolio Combination (After Ranking)
docs.text:
In Rust API:

Rust
let mom_weights = backtest(&mom_plan)?;
let rev_weights = backtest(&rev_plan)?;

// Combine portfolio weights
let combined = 0.6 * mom_weights + 0.4 * rev_weights;

docs.title: By Position
docs.text:
Track contribution of each position:

Rust
let attribution = AttributionAnalyzer::new();
let result = attribution.by_position(&returns, &weights);

docs.title: By Factor
docs.text:
Decompose returns by factor exposure:

Rust
attribution.add_factor("momentum", mom_returns);
attribution.add_factor("value", value_returns);
let result = attribution.analyze(&portfolio_returns)?;

See Returns Attribution for details.

docs.title: 1. Use Ranking
docs.text:
Ranking is robust to signal scale and outliers:

Text Only
// Good
weights = rank(signal).long_short(...)

// Risky (sensitive to outliers)
weights = signal.long_short(...)

docs.title: 2. Apply Position Caps
docs.text:
Prevent concentration risk:

Text Only
weights = rank(signal).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2, cap=0.05)

docs.title: 3. Consider Turnover
docs.text:
High turnover = high costs:

Text Only
// Lower turnover: longer lookback
signal slow_momentum:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, 60))  // 60-day lookback

// Higher turnover: shorter lookback
signal fast_momentum:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, 5))   // 5-day lookback

docs.title: 4. Match Rebalancing to Signal Horizon
docs.text:
• Fast signals → frequent rebalancing
  • Slow signals → infrequent rebalancing

docs.title: 5. Model Costs Realistically
docs.text: Include all costs: commissions, spread, impact, borrowing.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Backtesting Fundamentals - Simulation methodology
  • Transaction Costs - Cost modeling
  • Portfolio Constraints - Advanced constraints

docs.title: What Are Signals?
docs.text: Signals are the foundation of quantitative trading strategies in sigc. This page explains what signals are, how they work, and how to build them.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
A signal is a numerical score computed for each asset at each point in time. Higher scores indicate more attractive assets; lower scores indicate less attractive ones.

Text Only
signal momentum:
  returns = ret(prices, 20)
  score = zscore(returns)
  emit score

docs.title: Cross-Sectional
docs.text:
Signals rank assets relative to each other at each point in time:

Text Only
Date: 2024-01-15
  AAPL:  +1.2  (high score - attractive)
  MSFT:  +0.5
  GOOGL: -0.3
  AMZN:  -1.4  (low score - unattractive)

docs.title: Time-Varying
docs.text:
Signal values change as new data arrives:

Text Only
         AAPL    MSFT    GOOGL   AMZN
2024-01: +1.2    +0.5    -0.3    -1.4
2024-02: +0.8    +1.1    +0.2    -2.1
2024-03: -0.5    +0.9    +1.3    -1.7

docs.title: Normalized
docs.text:
Well-designed signals are normalized (mean ≈ 0, std ≈ 1):

Text Only
signal good:
  raw = ret(prices, 20)
  emit zscore(raw)  // Normalized: mean=0, std=1

docs.title: Factor-Based Signals
docs.text:
Based on academic research and economic intuition:

Factor Description Example Momentum Past returns predict future 12-1 month returns Value Cheap assets outperform Book-to-market ratio Quality High-quality outperforms ROE, earnings stability Size Small-cap premium Market cap (inverted) Volatility Low-vol anomaly Historical volatility Text Only
signal momentum:
  r12 = ret(prices, 252)  // 12-month return
  r1 = ret(prices, 21)    // 1-month return
  mom = r12 - r1          // Skip recent month
  emit zscore(mom)

signal value:
  emit zscore(book_to_market)

signal quality:
  emit zscore(roe)

docs.title: Technical Signals
docs.text:
Based on price patterns and indicators:

Text Only
signal rsi_signal:
  rsi_val = rsi(prices, 14)
  centered = rsi_val - 50
  emit -centered / 25  // Contrarian: buy oversold

signal macd_signal:
  emit macd(prices, 12, 26, 9)

signal trend:
  fast = ema(prices, 10)
  slow = ema(prices, 50)
  emit zscore(fast - slow)

docs.title: Statistical Signals
docs.text:
Based on statistical relationships:

Text Only
signal mean_reversion:
  ma = rolling_mean(prices, 20)
  std = rolling_std(prices, 20)
  z = (prices - ma) / std
  emit -zscore(z)  // Fade extremes

signal residual_momentum:
  // Market-neutral momentum
  market_ret = rolling_mean(ret(prices, 1), 20)
  stock_ret = ret(prices, 20)
  residual = stock_ret - market_ret
  emit zscore(residual)

docs.title: Step 1: Start with Raw Data
docs.text:
Text Only
signal step1:
  raw = ret(prices, 20)
  emit raw  // Not normalized - bad!

docs.title: Step 2: Normalize Cross-Sectionally
docs.text:
Text Only
signal step2:
  raw = ret(prices, 20)
  normalized = zscore(raw)
  emit normalized  // Better - mean=0, std=1

docs.title: Step 3: Handle Outliers
docs.text:
Text Only
signal step3:
  raw = ret(prices, 20)
  normalized = zscore(raw)
  cleaned = winsor(normalized, p=0.01)
  emit cleaned  // Best - outliers clipped

docs.title: Step 4: Adjust for Volatility
docs.text:
Text Only
signal step4:
  raw = ret(prices, 20)
  vol = rolling_std(ret(prices, 1), 60)
  vol_adj = raw / vol
  normalized = zscore(vol_adj)
  cleaned = winsor(normalized, p=0.01)
  emit cleaned  // High-vol stocks don't dominate

docs.title: Information Coefficient (IC)
docs.text:
The correlation between signal and future returns:

  • IC > 0.05: Good predictive signal
  • IC ≈ 0: No predictive power
  • IC < 0: Negative (contrarian) signal

docs.title: Turnover
docs.text:
How much the signal changes over time:

  • Low turnover: Stable signal, lower trading costs
  • High turnover: Responsive signal, higher trading costs

docs.title: Decay
docs.text:
How quickly the signal's predictive power fades:

Text Only
// Short-horizon signal (decays quickly)
signal short_term:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, 5))

// Long-horizon signal (slower decay)
signal long_term:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, 252))

docs.title: Linear Combination
docs.text:
Text Only
signal combo:
  mom = zscore(ret(prices, 60))
  rev = -zscore(ret(prices, 5))
  vol = -zscore(rolling_std(ret(prices, 1), 20))

  // Weight by confidence/research
  combined = 0.5 * mom + 0.3 * rev + 0.2 * vol
  emit combined

docs.title: Dynamic Weighting
docs.text:
Text Only
signal adaptive:
  mom = zscore(ret(prices, 60))
  rev = -zscore(ret(prices, 5))

  // More momentum when trending
  trend_strength = abs(rolling_mean(ret(prices, 1), 20))
  mom_weight = where(trend_strength > 0.01, 0.7, 0.3)

  emit mom_weight * mom + (1 - mom_weight) * rev

docs.title: Momentum with Skip
docs.text:
Skip recent returns to avoid short-term reversal:

Text Only
signal momentum_skip:
  total = ret(prices, 252)
  recent = ret(prices, 21)
  emit zscore(total - recent)

docs.title: Sector Neutralization
docs.text:
Remove sector bias:

Text Only
signal sector_neutral:
  raw = zscore(ret(prices, 20))
  neutral = neutralize(raw, by=sector)
  emit neutral

docs.title: Volatility Targeting
docs.text:
Scale by volatility:

Text Only
signal vol_target:
  raw = ret(prices, 20)
  vol = rolling_std(ret(prices, 1), 60)
  target_vol = 0.15  // 15% annualized
  scaled = raw * (target_vol / vol)
  emit zscore(scaled)

docs.title: Check Distribution
docs.text:
A well-behaved signal should have:

  • Mean ≈ 0
  • Std ≈ 1
  • Few extreme values

docs.title: Check Coverage
docs.text:
Ensure signal is computed for all assets:

Text Only
signal with_coverage:
  raw = ret(prices, 20)
  // Handle missing data
  filled = fill_nan(raw, 0)
  emit zscore(filled)

docs.title: Check Stability
docs.text:
Highly unstable signals are suspicious:

Text Only
// Compare signal values day-over-day
signal_change = abs(today_signal - yesterday_signal)
// If change is very large, investigate

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always normalize - Use zscore for cross-sectional comparability
  2. Handle outliers - Use winsor to clip extremes
  3. Adjust for volatility - Don't let high-vol stocks dominate
  4. Consider turnover - Balance responsiveness with trading costs
  5. Document your logic - Explain the economic rationale
  6. Test out-of-sample - Don't overfit to historical data

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Portfolio Construction - Convert signals to weights
  • Operators Reference - All available operators
  • Tutorials - Build complete strategies

docs.title: Type System
docs.text: sigc's type system catches errors at compile time, before expensive backtests run.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The type system has three components:

  1. Data Types (DType): float64, int32, bool, string, date
  2. Shapes: Scalar, vector, matrix dimensions
  3. Semantic Categories: Time-series, cross-sectional, panel
Text Only
signal example:
  prices    // Shape: (T, N) - panel
  ret_20 = ret(prices, 20)  // Shape: (T, N) - panel (time-series op)
  z = zscore(ret_20)        // Shape: (T, N) - panel (cross-sectional op)
  emit z

docs.title: Data Types
docs.text: DType Description Example Float64 Double-precision float Prices, returns Float32 Single-precision float Memory-optimized data Int64 64-bit integer Counts, IDs Int32 32-bit integer Small integers Bool Boolean Filters, conditions String Text Symbols, sectors Date Calendar date Trading dates DateTime Date and time Timestamps

docs.title: Shapes
docs.text:
Shapes describe the dimensionality of data:

Shape Dimensions Example Scalar () Single value: 20, 0.01 Vector (N,) Asset scores at one time Matrix (T, N) Full price panel

docs.title: Shape Inference
docs.text:
The compiler infers shapes from operations:

Text Only
signal shapes:
  prices        // (T, N) - loaded data
  ret_5 = ret(prices, 5)    // (T, N) - time-series preserves shape
  mean = rolling_mean(ret_5, 20)  // (T, N)
  z = zscore(mean)          // (T, N) - cross-sectional preserves T
  emit z

docs.title: Semantic Categories
docs.text:
Beyond shape, sigc tracks what kind of data each value represents:

Category Description Operators TimeSeries Per-asset over time ret, lag, rolling_*, ema CrossSection All assets at one time zscore, rank, winsor Panel Full time × asset matrix Most operations Scalar Single value 20, 0.01 Boolean True/false >, <, and, or

docs.title: Why Categories Matter
docs.text:
Prevents meaningless operations:

Text Only
// ERROR: Can't apply time-series operator to cross-sectional data
signal bad:
  returns = ret(prices, 20)     // TimeSeries operation
  z = zscore(returns)           // CrossSection operation - OK
  lagged_z = lag(z, 5)          // TimeSeries on CrossSection result - OK
  emit lagged_z

docs.title: Operator Signatures
docs.text:
Each operator has a signature defining valid inputs and outputs:

Text Only
zscore(x: CrossSection | Panel) -> CrossSection | Panel
  - Input: Numeric series (cross-sectional or panel)
  - Output: Same shape, z-scored across assets

ret(x: TimeSeries | Panel, n: Scalar) -> TimeSeries | Panel
  - Input: Time-series data, lookback period
  - Output: Same shape, returns computed

rank(x: CrossSection | Panel) -> CrossSection | Panel
  - Input: Numeric scores
  - Output: Ranks from 0 to 1

docs.title: Example Signatures
docs.text: Operator Input Output Notes ret(x, n) Panel, int Panel Time-series lag(x, n) Panel, int Panel Time-series zscore(x) Panel Panel Cross-sectional rank(x) Panel Panel Cross-sectional rolling_mean(x, n) Panel, int Panel Time-series where(c, a, b) Bool, Any, Any Any Conditional

docs.title: Type Errors
docs.text: The compiler reports type errors with helpful messages:

docs.title: Wrong Number of Arguments
docs.text:
Text Only
signal bad:
  x = zscore(prices, 20)  // Error!
Text Only
Error: zscore takes 1 argument, got 2
  --> strategy.sig:3:7
    |
  3 |   x = zscore(prices, 20)
    |       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
    |
help: zscore(x) takes a single series argument

docs.title: Type Mismatch
docs.text:
Text Only
signal bad:
  x = ret(prices, "twenty")  // Error!
Text Only
Error: Expected numeric, got String
  --> strategy.sig:3:19
    |
  3 |   x = ret(prices, "twenty")
    |                   ^^^^^^^^
    |
help: ret(x, n) requires numeric lookback period
      try: ret(prices, 20)

docs.title: Unknown Identifier
docs.text:
Text Only
signal bad:
  x = zscore(prces)  // Typo!
Text Only
Error: Unknown identifier 'prces'
  --> strategy.sig:3:14
    |
  3 |   x = zscore(prces)
    |              ^^^^^
    |
help: Did you mean 'prices'?

docs.title: Type Annotations
docs.text:
Usually, types are inferred. But you can add annotations for clarity:

Text Only
// In macros, parameter types are required
macro momentum(px: expr, lookback: number = 20):
  let r = ret(px, lookback)
  emit zscore(r)

docs.title: Parameter Types
docs.text: Type Description expr Any expression number Numeric value (int or float) string String literal ident Identifier name

docs.title: Type Inference
docs.text:
The compiler infers types through the computation graph:

Text Only
signal inferred:
  // prices: Panel<Float64, (T, N)>
  prices

  // returns: Panel<Float64, (T, N)>
  // Inferred from ret() signature
  returns = ret(prices, 20)

  // z: Panel<Float64, (T, N)>
  // Inferred from zscore() signature
  z = zscore(returns)

  emit z

docs.title: Inference Rules
docs.text:
1. Literals: Type from value (20 → Int64, 0.5 → Float64)
  2. Data Sources: Type from schema (CSV → Float64 by default)
  3. Operators: Output type from signature
  4. Arithmetic: Wider type wins (Int32 + Float64 → Float64)

docs.title: 1. Catch Errors Early
docs.text:
Text Only
// Caught at compile time, not after a 2-hour backtest
signal bad:
  x = unknownfunction(prices)

docs.title: 2. Better Error Messages
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Unknown function 'unknownfunction'
help: Did you mean 'rolling_mean' or 'rolling_std'?

docs.title: 3. IDE Support
docs.text:
The type system enables:

  • Hover documentation
  • Code completion
  • Go to definition
  • Refactoring

docs.title: 4. Optimization
docs.text:
The compiler uses types to:

  • Choose efficient implementations
  • Parallelize safe operations
  • Eliminate redundant computations

docs.title: Check Types in IDE
docs.text:
Hover over any expression to see its type:

Text Only
z = zscore(returns)
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
    zscore(x: Panel<Float64, (T, N)>) -> Panel<Float64, (T, N)>

docs.title: Use Explicit Types When Unclear
docs.text:
Text Only
// In functions, specify parameter types
fn volatility(x: expr, window: number = 20):
  rolling_std(ret(x, 1), window)

docs.title: Handle Missing Data
docs.text:
Use appropriate null handling:

Text Only
signal with_nulls:
  raw = ret(prices, 20)
  // fill_nan has signature: (x: Panel, value: Scalar) -> Panel
  filled = fill_nan(raw, 0)
  emit zscore(filled)

docs.title: Conditional Types
docs.text:
where returns the common type of its branches:

Text Only
signal conditional:
  condition = prices > lag(prices, 1)  // Bool
  result = where(condition, 1, -1)     // Int64
  emit result

docs.title: Type Coercion
docs.text:
Numeric types are coerced when needed:

Text Only
signal coerced:
  x = 5        // Int64
  y = 3.14     // Float64
  z = x + y    // Float64 (coerced)
  emit z

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Architecture - System design
  • Language Reference - Full DSL documentation
  • Operators - Operator signatures

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Help improve sigc! We welcome contributions.

docs.title: Ways to Contribute
docs.text: Contribution Description Bug Reports Report issues you find Feature Requests Suggest improvements Code Submit pull requests Documentation Improve docs Strategies Share example strategies

docs.title: 1. Fork the Repository
docs.text:
Bash
# Fork on GitHub, then clone
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/sigc.git
cd sigc

docs.title: 2. Set Up Development Environment
docs.text:
Bash
# Install Rust
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh

# Build
cargo build

# Run tests
cargo test

See Development Setup for details.

docs.title: 3. Create a Branch
docs.text:
Bash
git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name

docs.title: 4. Make Changes
docs.text: Follow our Code Style guide.

docs.title: 5. Test
docs.text:
Bash
cargo test
cargo clippy
cargo fmt --check

See Testing for details.

docs.title: 6. Submit PR
docs.text: Push and open a pull request.

docs.title: Before Reporting
docs.text:
1. Check existing issues
  2. Try latest version
  3. Reproduce the bug

docs.title: Bug Report Template
docs.text:
Markdown
## Description
Brief description of the bug.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Expected Behavior
What should happen.

## Actual Behavior
What actually happens.

## Environment
- sigc version:
- OS:
- Rust version:

## Additional Context
Any other information.

docs.title: Request Template
docs.text:
Markdown
## Problem
What problem does this solve?

## Proposed Solution
How would this feature work?

## Alternatives Considered
Other approaches you considered.

## Additional Context
Examples, mockups, etc.

docs.title: What We're Looking For
docs.text:
• Bug fixes
  • Performance improvements
  • New operators
  • Documentation improvements
  • Test coverage

docs.title: PR Requirements
docs.text:
• Tests pass
  • Code is formatted (cargo fmt)
  • No clippy warnings
  • Documentation updated
  • Commit messages are clear

docs.title: Commit Messages
docs.text:
Text Only
type(scope): description

Types: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore

Examples: Text Only

feat(operators): add bollinger band operator
fix(parser): handle empty signal blocks
docs(tutorial): add momentum strategy example

docs.title: Improving Docs
docs.text:
1. Edit files in documentation/docs/
  2. Preview with mkdocs serve
  3. Submit PR

docs.title: Writing Guide
docs.text:
• Use clear, simple language
  • Include code examples
  • Add diagrams where helpful
  • Link to related topics

See Documentation Guide.

docs.title: Strategy Examples
docs.text:
Share your strategies:

  1. Add to strategies/ directory
  2. Include comments explaining the approach
  3. Add documentation in docs/strategies/

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: We follow the Rust Code of Conduct.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Be welcoming and inclusive
  • Be respectful and constructive
  • Be collaborative

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:

  • Issues

docs.title: Documentation Index
docs.text:

  • Code Style - Coding standards

docs.title: Code Style Guide
docs.text: Coding standards for sigc contributions.

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text:
Use rustfmt for all Rust code:

Bash

docs.title: Naming Conventions

pub fn compute_signal(date: NaiveDate, prices: &DataFrame) -> Result<f64> {
    // ...

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Use custom error types:

Rust
// Good
fn load_data(path: &str) -> Result<DataFrame, DataError> {
    let file = File::open(path)
        .map_err(|e| DataError::FileNotFound(path.to_string()))?;
    // ...
}

// Avoid
fn load_data(path: &str) -> Result<DataFrame, Box<dyn Error>> {
    // ...
}

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Test all public functions:

Rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_compute_signal() {
        let prices = create_test_data();
        let result = compute_signal(date, &prices).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(result, expected);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_compute_signal_missing_date() {
        let prices = create_test_data();
        let result = compute_signal(missing_date, &prices);
        assert!(result.is_err());
    }
}

docs.title: Signal Definitions
docs.text:
Text Only
// Good: Clear, descriptive names
signal momentum_12_1:
  ret_12m = ret(prices, 252)
  ret_1m = ret(prices, 21)
  emit zscore(ret_12m - ret_1m)

// Avoid: Cryptic names
signal s1:
  r = ret(prices, 252) - ret(prices, 21)
  emit zscore(r)

def compute_signal(data: pd.DataFrame, lookback: int) -> pd.Series:
    """Compute the momentum signal.

    Args:
        data: Price data with 'close' column
        lookback: Number of days for return calculation

    Returns:
        Series of signal values

    Raises:

    """

docs.title: Basic Test
Test Error Cases

Writing Integration Tests
docs.text:
Create files in tests/:

Rust
// tests/backtest_integration.rs

use sigc::Strategy;

#[test]
fn test_momentum_backtest() {
    let strategy = Strategy::from_file("strategies/momentum.sig").unwrap();
    let results = strategy.run().unwrap();

    assert!(results.sharpe_ratio() > 0.0);
    assert!(results.total_return() > -1.0);
}

tests/fixtures/

docs.title: Load Fixtures
Property-Based Testing
docs.text:
Use proptest for property-based tests:

Rust
use proptest::prelude::*;

proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn test_zscore_mean_zero(values in prop::collection::vec(any::<f64>(), 10..100)) {
        let zscores = zscore(&values);
        let mean: f64 = zscores.iter().sum::<f64>() / zscores.len() as f64;
        prop_assert!((mean - 0.0).abs() < 1e-10);
    }
}

docs.title: Mocking
docs.text:
For external dependencies:

Rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use mockall::predicate::*;
    use super::*;

    mock! {
        BrokerClient {
            fn submit_order(&self, order: Order) -> Result<()>;
        }
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_execution() {
        let mut mock_broker = MockBrokerClient::new();
        mock_broker
            .expect_submit_order()
            .times(1)
            .returning(|_| Ok(()));

        let executor = Executor::new(mock_broker);
        let result = executor.execute(order);
        assert!(result.is_ok());
    }
}

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text: Format Extension Best For CSV .csv Small datasets, debugging Parquet .parquet Large datasets, production S3 s3:// Cloud storage PostgreSQL postgresql:// Database integration

docs.title: CSV

Text Only

  source = "prices.csv"
  format = csv

    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices
    volume: Numeric

Text Only
data:
  source = "..."           # Required: file path, URL, or connection string

  columns:                 # Column definitions (required for CSV)
    column_name: Type [as alias]
  options:                 # Optional: format-specific settings

docs.title: Column Types
docs.text: Type Description Example Date Date column (index) 2024-01-15 Symbol Asset identifier AAPL, MSFT Numeric Price, volume, etc. 150.25 String Text data "Technology"

docs.title: Column Aliasing
docs.text:
Rename columns for cleaner code:

Text Only
data:
  source = "raw_data.csv"
  format = csv
  columns:
    trade_date: Date
    symbol: Symbol
    adj_close: Numeric as prices      # Use 'prices' in signals
    shares_traded: Numeric as volume  # Use 'volume' in signals

docs.title: Multiple Data Sources
docs.text:
Combine data from multiple files:

Text Only
data prices:
  source = "prices.parquet"
  format = parquet

data fundamentals:
  source = "fundamentals.parquet"
  format = parquet

signal combined:
  momentum = zscore(ret(prices.close, 60))
  value = zscore(fundamentals.book_to_market)
  emit 0.5 * momentum + 0.5 * value

docs.title: 1. Use Parquet for Production
docs.text:
Text Only
// Parquet is 5-10x faster than CSV for large datasets
data:
  source = "prices.parquet"
  format = parquet

docs.title: 2. Define Types Explicitly
docs.text:
Text Only
// Good: explicit types

  date: Date
  ticker: Symbol
  close: Numeric as prices

// Bad: relying on inference

docs.title: 3. Handle Missing Data
docs.text:
Text Only
signal robust:
  // Fill missing prices before computation
  clean = fill_nan(prices, 0)
  emit zscore(ret(clean, 20))

docs.text:
Text Only

columns:
  px_last: Numeric as prices
  px_volume: Numeric as volume
  px_high: Numeric as high
  px_low: Numeric as low

docs.title: Corporate Actions
docs.text: Handling stock splits, dividends, and other corporate actions in your data.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Corporate actions affect price and volume data:

Action Effect on Price Effect on Volume Stock Split Divided Multiplied Reverse Split Multiplied Divided Dividend Reduced (ex-date) No change Spin-off Reduced No change Merger Converted/Delisted Varies

docs.title: Unadjusted (Raw) Prices
docs.text:
What actually traded on each day:

Text Only
Date       | AAPL Price
2014-06-06 | $645.57    <- Before 7:1 split
2014-06-09 | $93.70     <- After split

docs.title: Adjusted Prices
docs.text:
Retroactively adjusted for corporate actions:

Text Only
Date       | AAPL Adjusted Price
2014-06-06 | $92.22     <- Adjusted back
2014-06-09 | $93.70     <- Same

Always use adjusted prices for backtesting.

docs.title: Data Provider
docs.text:
Most data providers offer adjusted prices:

Text Only
data:
  source = "adjusted_prices.parquet"
  format = parquet
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    adj_close: Numeric as prices  # Use adjusted close
    volume: Numeric

docs.title: Manual Adjustment
docs.text:
If you have split data:

Text Only
signal adjust_for_splits:
  // Cumulative adjustment factor
  adj_factor = cumprod(split_ratio)

  // Adjust prices backward
  adjusted_price = prices / adj_factor

  emit adjusted_price

docs.title: Detection
docs.text:
Detect splits in unadjusted data:

Text Only
signal detect_splits:
  daily_return = ret(prices, 1)

  // Large drop (potential split)
  potential_split = daily_return < -0.4

  // Check if round ratio
  ratio = lag(prices, 1) / prices
  is_round = abs(round(ratio) - ratio) < 0.01

  split_detected = potential_split and is_round

  emit where(split_detected, ratio, 1)

docs.title: Common Split Ratios
docs.text: Ratio Description 2:1 Price halves 3:1 Price divides by 3 4:1 Price divides by 4 7:1 Apple 2014 split 10:1 NVIDIA 2024 split 20:1 Google/Amazon 2022 splits

docs.title: Reverse Splits
docs.text:
Text Only
signal detect_reverse_split:
  daily_return = ret(prices, 1)

  // Large jump (potential reverse split)
  potential_reverse = daily_return > 1.0

  // Check if round ratio
  ratio = prices / lag(prices, 1)
  is_round = abs(round(ratio) - ratio) < 0.01

  reverse_detected = potential_reverse and is_round

  emit where(reverse_detected, ratio, 1)

docs.title: Dividend Adjustment
docs.text:
Prices drop by dividend amount on ex-date:

Text Only
signal adjust_for_dividends:
  // Dividend yield on ex-date
  div_yield = dividend / lag(prices, 1)

  // Cumulative adjustment factor
  adj_factor = cumprod(1 - div_yield)

  // Adjust prices backward
  adjusted_price = prices / adj_factor

  emit adjusted_price

docs.title: Total Return
docs.text:
Include dividends in return calculation:

Text Only
signal total_return:
  price_return = ret(prices, 1)
  div_return = dividend / lag(prices, 1)
  total = price_return + div_return
  emit total

docs.title: Spin-offs
docs.text:
When a company splits into two:

Text Only
signal handle_spinoff:
  // Parent company price drops
  // Spinoff creates new security

  // Track combined value
  parent_value = parent_price * parent_shares
  spinoff_value = spinoff_price * spinoff_shares
  total_value = parent_value + spinoff_value

  emit total_value

docs.title: Cash Acquisition
docs.text:
Target is acquired for cash:

Text Only
signal handle_cash_acquisition:
  // Stock delists at acquisition price
  was_trading = lag(prices, 1) > 0
  not_trading = prices == 0 or is_nan(prices)
  acquired = was_trading and not_trading

  // Last price should be ~acquisition price
  emit where(acquired, 0, prices)

docs.title: Stock-for-Stock Merger
docs.text:
Target converted to acquirer shares:

Text Only
signal handle_stock_merger:
  // Target shareholders receive exchange_ratio shares of acquirer
  target_value = target_price * exchange_ratio

  emit target_value

docs.title: Validation
Volume Adjustment
docs.text:
When prices are adjusted, volume should be adjusted inversely:

Text Only
// Adjusted volume (for split-adjusted prices)
adjusted_volume = volume * split_ratio

docs.title: 1. Use Pre-Adjusted Data
docs.text:
Most data providers handle adjustments:

Text Only
data:
  source = "bloomberg_adjusted.parquet"  # Pre-adjusted
  format = parquet

docs.title: 2. Verify Adjustments
3. Track Adjustment Factors
docs.text:
Keep adjustment factors for audit:

Text Only
data:
  source = "prices.parquet"
  format = parquet
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as raw_prices
    adj_close: Numeric as prices
    adj_factor: Numeric  # For debugging

docs.title: 4. Handle Delistings
docs.text:
Text Only
signal handle_delisting:
  // Zero out signals for delisted stocks
  is_trading = prices > 0 and not(is_nan(prices))
  signal = where(is_trading, raw_signal, 0)
  emit signal

docs.title: 5. Document Your Adjustments
Missing Split Adjustment
docs.text:
Text Only
Symptom: -50%, -67% returns on split dates
Solution: Use adjusted prices or apply split factors

docs.title: Missing Dividend Adjustment
docs.text:
Text Only
Symptom: Small drops on ex-dividend dates
Solution: Use dividend-adjusted prices or total return prices

docs.title: Inconsistent Adjustment
docs.text:
Text Only
Symptom: Some prices adjusted, others not
Solution: Verify entire dataset uses same adjustment method

docs.title: Volume Not Adjusted
docs.text:
Text Only
Symptom: Volume spikes/drops on split dates
Solution: Adjust volume inversely to price adjustment

docs.title: Adjustment Timeline
docs.text:
Text Only
Original Data:
2024-01-15: $200 (before 2:1 split)
2024-01-16: $100 (after split)

Adjusted Data (looking back):
2024-01-15: $100 (adjusted)
2024-01-16: $100 (actual)

When loading in 2024:
- All historical prices adjusted to current shares outstanding
- Returns are meaningful across corporate actions
