  source = "prices.csv"
  format = csv
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices
    volume: Numeric

docs.title: Wide Format
docs.text:
One row per date, symbols as columns:

Text Only
date,AAPL,MSFT,GOOGL
2024-01-02,185.64,374.51,140.21
2024-01-03,184.25,372.89,139.75

Load with pivot option:

Text Only
data:
  source = "prices_wide.csv"
  format = csv
  options:
    pivot = true
    date_column = "date"

docs.title: Required Columns
docs.text:
Text Only
columns:
  date: Date      # Index column
  ticker: Symbol  # Asset identifier

docs.title: Data Columns
docs.text:
Text Only
columns:
  close: Numeric as prices    # Closing price
  volume: Numeric             # Trading volume
  high: Numeric               # Daily high
  low: Numeric                # Daily low
  open: Numeric               # Opening price

docs.title: Optional Metadata
docs.text:
Text Only
columns:
  sector: String              # Sector classification
  industry: String            # Industry classification
  market_cap: Numeric         # Market capitalization

docs.title: Column Aliasing
docs.text:
Rename columns for consistency:

Text Only
data:
  source = "bloomberg_data.csv"
  format = csv
  columns:
    PX_LAST: Numeric as prices
    PX_VOLUME: Numeric as volume
    PX_HIGH: Numeric as high
    PX_LOW: Numeric as low

Use in signals:

Text Only
signal example:
  // Use aliased name 'prices' instead of 'PX_LAST'
  emit zscore(ret(prices, 20))

docs.title: Date Parsing
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data.csv"
  format = csv
  columns:
    date: Date
    ...
  options:
    date_format = "%Y-%m-%d"       # ISO format (default)
    # date_format = "%m/%d/%Y"     # US format
    # date_format = "%d/%m/%Y"     # European format

docs.title: Delimiter
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data.tsv"
  format = csv
  options:
    delimiter = "\t"   # Tab-separated
    # delimiter = ";"  # Semicolon

docs.title: Header
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "no_header.csv"
  format = csv
  options:
    has_header = false
  columns:
    col1: Date
    col2: Symbol
    col3: Numeric as prices

docs.title: Encoding
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data.csv"
  format = csv
  options:
    encoding = "utf-8"   # Default
    # encoding = "latin1"

docs.title: Skip Rows
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data_with_notes.csv"
  format = csv
  options:
    skip_rows = 3   # Skip first 3 rows

docs.title: Type Inference
docs.text:
sigc can infer types, but explicit is better:

Text Only
// Explicit (recommended)
columns:
  date: Date
  ticker: Symbol
  close: Numeric as prices
  volume: Numeric

// Inferred (less reliable)
data:
  source = "data.csv"
  format = csv
  options:
    infer_types = true

docs.title: Single Directory
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data/*.csv"
  format = csv
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices

docs.title: Explicit List
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = ["prices_2023.csv", "prices_2024.csv"]
  format = csv
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices

docs.title: In CSV
docs.text:
Text Only
date,ticker,close,volume
2024-01-02,AAPL,185.64,45678900
2024-01-02,MSFT,,23456789         # Missing price
2024-01-03,AAPL,184.25,           # Missing volume

docs.title: In sigc
File Size Guidelines
docs.text: Size Rows Recommendation Small < 100K CSV is fine Medium 100K - 1M Consider Parquet Large > 1M Use Parquet

docs.title: Optimization Tips
docs.text:
Text Only
// 1. Use specific columns (don't load unused data)
columns:
  date: Date
  ticker: Symbol
  close: Numeric as prices  # Only load what you need

// 2. Convert to Parquet for repeated use
// Run once: sigc convert data.csv data.parquet

// 3. Use date filtering
options:
  start_date = "2020-01-01"
  end_date = "2024-12-31"

docs.title: Date Parsing Errors
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Failed to parse date '01/15/2024'

Fix: Specify date format:

  date_format = "%m/%d/%Y"

docs.title: Missing Column
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Column 'prices' not found

Fix: Check column names or add alias:

Text Only

  close: Numeric as prices  # 'close' in CSV, 'prices' in code

docs.title: Encoding Issues
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Invalid UTF-8 sequence

Fix: Specify encoding:

  encoding = "latin1"

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Parquet Format - Faster loading for large datasets
  • Data Quality - Validating your data
  • Signal Section - Using loaded data

docs.title: Data Quality
docs.text: Ensuring your data is clean and reliable for backtesting.

docs.title: Missing Data
docs.text:
• Gaps: Missing trading days
  • NaN values: Missing prices or volumes
  • Incomplete symbols: Assets that appear/disappear

docs.title: Data Errors
docs.text:
• Spikes: Erroneous price jumps
  • Zeros: Invalid zero prices
  • Stale data: Unchanged values when they should change

docs.title: Structural Issues
docs.text:
• Survivorship bias: Only successful companies in dataset
  • Look-ahead bias: Using future information
  • Corporate actions: Unadjusted splits and dividends

docs.title: Check for NaN
docs.text:
Text Only
signal check_missing:
  // Count missing values
  has_price = where(is_nan(prices), 0, 1)
  has_volume = where(is_nan(volume), 0, 1)

  // Quality score per asset
  quality = (has_price + has_volume) / 2

  emit quality

docs.title: Missing Data Report
docs.text:
Bash
sigc check data.parquet --missing

Output:

Text Only
Missing Data Report:
===================
Column     | Missing | Pct Missing
-----------+---------+-----------
close      |    145  |    0.02%
volume     |    892  |    0.12%
high       |    145  |    0.02%
low        |    145  |    0.02%

Assets with >5% missing: DELISTED1, DELISTED2

docs.title: Fill with Zero
docs.text:
Text Only
signal fill_zero:
  clean_prices = fill_nan(prices, 0)
  emit zscore(ret(clean_prices, 20))

docs.title: Forward Fill
docs.text:
Text Only
signal forward_fill:
  // Use previous value
  filled = where(is_nan(prices), lag(prices, 1), prices)
  emit filled

docs.title: Exclude Missing
docs.text:
Text Only
signal exclude_missing:
  valid = not(is_nan(prices))
  signal = where(valid, zscore(ret(prices, 20)), 0)
  emit signal

docs.title: Interpolation
docs.text:
Text Only
signal interpolate:
  // Linear interpolation
  prev = lag(prices, 1)
  next = lead(prices, 1)
  interpolated = where(is_nan(prices), (prev + next) / 2, prices)
  emit interpolated

docs.title: Statistical Detection
docs.text:
Text Only
signal outlier_detection:
  returns = ret(prices, 1)
  z = zscore(returns)

  // Flag returns > 5 standard deviations
  outlier = abs(z) > 5

  emit where(outlier, 1, 0)

docs.title: Price Spike Detection
docs.text:
Text Only
signal spike_detection:
  returns = ret(prices, 1)

  // Large single-day moves
  spike = abs(returns) > 0.5  // > 50% move

  emit where(spike, 1, 0)

docs.title: Volume Anomalies
docs.text:
Text Only
signal volume_anomaly:
  avg_volume = rolling_mean(volume, 20)
  ratio = volume / avg_volume

  // Volume > 10x average
  anomaly = ratio > 10

  emit where(anomaly, 1, 0)

docs.title: Winsorization
docs.text:
Text Only
signal winsorized:
  returns = ret(prices, 20)
  z = zscore(returns)

  // Clip at 1st and 99th percentile
  cleaned = winsor(z, p=0.01)

  emit cleaned

docs.title: Clipping
docs.text:
Text Only
signal clipped:
  returns = ret(prices, 20)
  z = zscore(returns)

  // Hard clip at ±3 standard deviations
  bounded = clip(z, -3, 3)

  emit bounded

docs.title: Exclusion
docs.text:
Text Only
signal exclude_outliers:
  returns = ret(prices, 20)
  z = zscore(returns)

  // Exclude extreme values
  valid = abs(z) < 5
  signal = where(valid, z, 0)

  emit signal

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Backtests that only include currently active securities overstate returns because failed companies are excluded.

docs.title: Solution 1: Point-in-Time Data
docs.text:
Use a data provider that includes delisted securities:

Text Only
data:
  source = "point_in_time_prices.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    include_delisted = true

docs.title: Solution 2: Dead Stock Handling
docs.text:
Text Only
signal handle_delisting:
  // Detect when a stock stops trading
  was_trading = lag(prices, 1) > 0
  not_trading = prices == 0 or is_nan(prices)
  delisted = was_trading and not_trading

  // Zero out signal for delisted stocks
  raw_signal = zscore(ret(prices, 60))
  signal = where(delisted, 0, raw_signal)

  emit signal

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Using information that wouldn't have been available at the time of the trade.

docs.title: Common Sources
docs.text:
1. Same-day data: Using close price to make decisions before market close
  2. Reported vs actual dates: Using data before it was actually reported
  3. Revisions: Using revised data instead of original releases

docs.title: Solution: Lag Your Data
docs.text:
Text Only
signal no_lookahead:
  // Use previous day's close for today's signal
  yesterday_price = lag(prices, 1)
  signal = zscore(ret(yesterday_price, 20))
  emit signal

docs.title: Fundamental Data
docs.text:
Text Only
signal fundamental_signal:
  // Earnings reported with delay
  // Use 1-quarter lag to be safe
  lagged_earnings = lag(earnings, 63)  // ~3 months
  signal = zscore(lagged_earnings / prices)
  emit signal

docs.title: Before Loading
docs.text:
• Check file format (CSV headers, Parquet schema)
  • Verify date range is complete
  • Confirm all expected symbols present
  • Check for duplicate rows

docs.title: After Loading
docs.text:
• Count missing values per column
  • Verify no future dates
  • Check price ranges are reasonable
  • Validate volume is non-negative

docs.title: Before Backtesting
docs.text:
• Confirm no look-ahead bias
  • Verify corporate actions adjusted
  • Check for survivorship bias
  • Test on subset of data first

docs.title: Completeness Score
docs.text:
Text Only
signal completeness:
  // What percentage of data is present?
  has_data = where(is_nan(prices), 0, 1)
  score = rolling_mean(has_data, 252)  // Over 1 year
  emit score

docs.title: Price Quality
docs.text:
Text Only
signal price_quality:
  // No zero or negative prices
  valid_price = prices > 0

  // No extreme daily returns
  ret_1d = ret(prices, 1)
  reasonable_return = abs(ret_1d) < 0.5

  quality = where(valid_price and reasonable_return, 1, 0)
  emit quality

docs.title: Volume Quality
docs.text:
Text Only
signal volume_quality:
  // No zero volume on trading days
  has_volume = volume > 0

  // No extreme volume spikes
  avg_vol = rolling_mean(volume, 20)
  reasonable_vol = volume < 50 * avg_vol

  quality = where(has_volume and reasonable_vol, 1, 0)
  emit quality

docs.title: sigc CLI
docs.text:
Bash
# Run all quality checks
sigc validate data.parquet

# Check specific issues
sigc validate data.parquet --check missing
sigc validate data.parquet --check outliers
sigc validate data.parquet --check duplicates

docs.title: In Strategy
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "prices.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    validate = true           # Enable validation
    max_missing_pct = 0.05    # Fail if >5% missing
    max_outlier_pct = 0.01    # Fail if >1% outliers

docs.title: 1. Always Validate New Data
docs.text:
Bash
sigc validate new_data.parquet --strict

docs.title: 2. Use Quality Thresholds
docs.text:
Text Only
signal with_quality_filter:
  // Only trade assets with good data
  quality = calculate_quality(prices, volume)
  raw_signal = zscore(ret(prices, 60))
  signal = where(quality > 0.95, raw_signal, 0)
  emit signal

docs.title: 3. Document Data Sources
4. Version Your Data
docs.text:
Text Only
data/
├── v1/
│   └── prices.parquet
├── v2/
│   └── prices.parquet  # Fixed outliers
└── v3/
    └── prices.parquet  # Added delisted stocks

docs.title: 5. Keep Raw and Cleaned Data
docs.text:
Text Only
// Load raw data
data raw:
  source = "prices_raw.parquet"

// Apply cleaning
signal clean:
  cleaned = winsor(zscore(raw.prices), p=0.01)
  emit cleaned

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Corporate Actions - Handling splits and dividends
  • CSV Format - Loading CSV files
  • Backtesting - Running backtests

docs.title: Parquet Format
docs.text: Apache Parquet is the recommended format for production data in sigc.

docs.title: Why Parquet?
docs.text: Feature CSV Parquet Read Speed Slow 5-10x faster File Size Large 50-90% smaller Type Safety No Yes Column Pruning No Yes Predicate Pushdown No Yes

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "prices.parquet"
  format = parquet

Types are automatically inferred from Parquet schema.

docs.title: Column Selection
docs.text:
Only load needed columns:

Text Only
data:
  source = "market_data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices
    volume: Numeric
  # Other columns in file are ignored

docs.title: Row Groups
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "large_file.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    row_group_size = 100000  # Rows per group

docs.title: Compression
docs.text:
sigc reads all standard compressions:

  • Snappy (default, fast)
  • Gzip (smaller files)
  • LZ4 (fastest)
  • Zstd (best compression ratio)

docs.title: Memory Mapping
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "huge_file.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    memory_map = true  # Memory-map instead of loading

docs.title: From CSV with sigc
docs.text:
Bash
# Convert CSV to Parquet
sigc convert prices.csv prices.parquet

docs.title: From Python
docs.text:
Python
import pandas as pd

# Read CSV
df = pd.read_csv('prices.csv', parse_dates=['date'])

# Write Parquet
df.to_parquet('prices.parquet', index=False)

docs.title: From Polars
docs.text:
Python
import polars as pl

# Read and write
df = pl.read_csv('prices.csv')
df.write_parquet('prices.parquet')

docs.title: Required Columns
docs.text:
Your Parquet file should have:

Text Only
date: Date (or Timestamp)
ticker/symbol: String
[price columns]: Float64

docs.title: Example Schema
Hive-Style Partitioning
docs.text:
Text Only
data/
├── year=2023/
│   ├── month=01/
│   │   └── data.parquet
│   └── month=02/
│       └── data.parquet
└── year=2024/
    └── month=01/
        └── data.parquet
Text Only
data:
  source = "data/"
  format = parquet
  options:
    partitioned = true

docs.title: Date Filtering with Partitions
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data/"
  format = parquet
  options:
    partitioned = true
    start_date = "2023-01-01"  # Only load needed partitions
    end_date = "2024-12-31"

docs.title: Directory
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data/*.parquet"
  format = parquet

docs.title: Explicit List
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = ["prices_2023.parquet", "prices_2024.parquet"]
  format = parquet

docs.title: Column Aliasing
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  columns:
    px_last: Numeric as prices
    px_volume: Numeric as volume

docs.title: Type Mapping
docs.text: Parquet Type sigc Type INT32 (DATE) Date INT64 (TIMESTAMP) Date STRING Symbol, String FLOAT, DOUBLE Numeric BOOLEAN Boolean

docs.title: 1. Column Pruning
docs.text:
Text Only
// Only load required columns
data:
  source = "large_file.parquet"
  format = parquet
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices  # Only loads 'close', not all columns

docs.title: 2. Predicate Pushdown
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    start_date = "2020-01-01"  # Filter pushed to reader
    symbols = ["AAPL", "MSFT", "GOOGL"]

docs.title: 3. Row Group Statistics
docs.text: Parquet stores min/max per row group, enabling fast filtering.

docs.title: 4. Memory Mapping for Large Files
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "100gb_file.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    memory_map = true  # Don't load entire file

docs.title: Compression Comparison
docs.text:
Compression Read Speed Write Speed Ratio None Fastest Fastest 1.0x Snappy Fast Fast 2-3x LZ4 Fast Fast 2-3x Gzip Medium Slow 4-6x Zstd Medium Medium 5-8x

Recommendation: Use Snappy for most cases (good balance).

docs.title: Example: Converting a Large Dataset
docs.text:
Python
import polars as pl

# Read CSV in chunks
df = pl.scan_csv('huge_prices.csv')

# Write as partitioned Parquet
df.sink_parquet(
    'prices/',
    compression='snappy',
    row_group_size=100_000,
    partition_by=['year', 'month']
)

docs.title: Schema Mismatch
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Schema mismatch in file 'data2.parquet'

Ensure all Parquet files have identical schemas when loading multiple files.

docs.title: Memory Issues
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Out of memory loading file

Solutions:

  1. Use memory mapping: memory_map = true
  2. Select only needed columns
  3. Use date filtering
  4. Partition large files

docs.title: Missing Columns
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Column 'prices' not found in Parquet schema

Check the actual schema:

Bash
# Using Python
python -c "import pyarrow.parquet as pq; print(pq.read_schema('file.parquet'))"

# Using sigc
sigc inspect file.parquet

docs.title: 1. Use Snappy Compression
docs.text:
Python
df.to_parquet('data.parquet', compression='snappy')

docs.title: 2. Choose Appropriate Row Group Size
docs.text:
• Small files (< 1M rows): 50K-100K rows
  • Large files: 100K-500K rows

docs.title: 3. Partition by Date for Time-Series
docs.text:
Text Only
data/
├── year=2023/
├── year=2024/

docs.title: 4. Include Metadata
docs.text:
Python
# Add custom metadata
import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq

table = pa.Table.from_pandas(df)
metadata = {
    b'source': b'Bloomberg',
    b'created': b'2024-01-15',
    b'frequency': b'daily'
}
table = table.replace_schema_metadata(metadata)
pq.write_table(table, 'data.parquet')

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• S3 Storage - Cloud storage integration
  • Data Quality - Validating your data
  • CSV Format - When CSV is appropriate

docs.title: PostgreSQL Integration
docs.text: Load data directly from PostgreSQL databases.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = "SELECT date, ticker, close, volume FROM daily_prices"

docs.title: Format
docs.text:
Text Only
postgresql://[user[:password]@]host[:port]/database[?options]

docs.title: Examples
Connection String (Development)
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://user:password@localhost/marketdb"
  query = "..."

docs.title: Environment Variables (Recommended)
docs.text:
Bash
export PGUSER=your_user
export PGPASSWORD=your_password
export PGHOST=localhost
export PGDATABASE=marketdb
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql:///marketdb"  # Uses env vars
  query = "..."

docs.title: Password File (~/.pgpass)
docs.text:
Text Only
# ~/.pgpass
hostname:port:database:username:password

docs.title: Basic Query
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = "SELECT date, ticker, close, volume FROM daily_prices"

docs.title: With Filters
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = """
    SELECT date, ticker, close, volume
    FROM daily_prices
    WHERE date >= '2020-01-01'
      AND ticker IN ('AAPL', 'MSFT', 'GOOGL')
    ORDER BY date, ticker
  """

docs.title: With Joins
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = """
    SELECT
      p.date,
      p.ticker,
      p.close,
      p.volume,
      f.pe_ratio,
      f.book_value
    FROM daily_prices p
    LEFT JOIN fundamentals f
      ON p.ticker = f.ticker
      AND p.date = f.date
    ORDER BY p.date, p.ticker
  """

docs.title: Using Parameters
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = """
    SELECT date, ticker, close, volume
    FROM daily_prices
    WHERE date BETWEEN $1 AND $2
  """
  options:
    params = ["2020-01-01", "2024-12-31"]

docs.title: Column Mapping
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = "SELECT trade_date, symbol, adj_close, shares_traded FROM prices"
  columns:
    trade_date: Date
    symbol: Symbol
    adj_close: Numeric as prices
    shares_traded: Numeric as volume

docs.title: SSL Mode
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://host/db"
  query = "..."
  options:
    sslmode = "require"  # disable, allow, prefer, require, verify-ca, verify-full

docs.title: Connection Pool
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://host/db"
  query = "..."
  options:
    pool_size = 5
    pool_timeout = 30

docs.title: Statement Timeout
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://host/db"
  query = "..."
  options:
    statement_timeout = 60000  # 60 seconds in milliseconds

docs.title: Index Usage
docs.text:
Ensure your tables have appropriate indexes:

SQL
-- Recommended indexes for market data
CREATE INDEX idx_prices_date ON daily_prices(date);
CREATE INDEX idx_prices_ticker ON daily_prices(ticker);
CREATE INDEX idx_prices_date_ticker ON daily_prices(date, ticker);

docs.title: Partitioned Tables
docs.text:
For large datasets, use PostgreSQL partitioning:

SQL
-- Partition by date range
CREATE TABLE daily_prices (
    date DATE NOT NULL,
    ticker TEXT NOT NULL,
    close NUMERIC,
    volume BIGINT
) PARTITION BY RANGE (date);

CREATE TABLE daily_prices_2023 PARTITION OF daily_prices
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2023-01-01') TO ('2024-01-01');

docs.title: Query Optimization
docs.text:
Text Only
// Use specific columns
query = "SELECT date, ticker, close FROM prices"  // Not SELECT *

// Add date filters
query = "SELECT ... WHERE date >= '2020-01-01'"

// Use LIMIT for testing
query = "SELECT ... LIMIT 1000"

docs.title: Parallel Queries
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://host/db"
  query = "..."
  options:
    parallel_workers = 4  # Enable parallel query

docs.title: Prices and Fundamentals
docs.text:
Text Only
data prices:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = "SELECT date, ticker, close FROM daily_prices"
  columns:
    close: Numeric as prices

data fundamentals:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = "SELECT date, ticker, pe_ratio, book_value FROM fundamentals"

signal combined:
  momentum = zscore(ret(prices.prices, 60))
  value = zscore(fundamentals.book_value)
  emit 0.5 * momentum + 0.5 * value

docs.title: Read Replica
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://read-replica.host/marketdb"
  query = "..."
  options:
    target_session_attrs = "read-only"

docs.title: TimescaleDB Support
docs.text:
sigc works with TimescaleDB hypertables:

SQL
-- Create hypertable
SELECT create_hypertable('daily_prices', 'date');
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://localhost/marketdb"
  query = """
    SELECT time_bucket('1 day', date) AS date,
           ticker,
           last(close, date) AS close,
           sum(volume) AS volume
    FROM daily_prices
    WHERE date >= '2020-01-01'
    GROUP BY 1, 2
    ORDER BY 1, 2
  """

docs.title: Connection Failed
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: connection refused

Check:

  1. PostgreSQL is running
  2. Host/port are correct
  3. Firewall allows connection

docs.title: Authentication Failed
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: password authentication failed

Check:

  1. Username/password correct
  2. User has database access
  3. pg_hba.conf allows connection

docs.title: Query Timeout
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: canceling statement due to statement timeout

Increase timeout:

Text Only
options:
  statement_timeout = 120000  # 2 minutes

docs.title: Permission Denied
1. Use Read Replicas for Analysis
docs.text:
Text Only
// Don't query production primary for backtesting
source = "postgresql://read-replica/db"

docs.title: 2. Add Date Filters
docs.text:
Text Only
query = """
  SELECT date, ticker, close, volume
  FROM daily_prices
  WHERE date >= '2020-01-01'  -- Always filter!
"""

docs.title: 3. Use Prepared Statements
docs.text:
Text Only
options:
  prepare = true

docs.title: 4. Set Reasonable Timeouts
docs.text:
Text Only
options:
  statement_timeout = 60000
  connect_timeout = 10

docs.title: 5. Close Connections
docs.text: sigc automatically manages connection pooling and cleanup.

docs.title: Example: Complete Setup
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "postgresql://analytics:${PGPASSWORD}@db.example.com:5432/marketdb"
  query = """
    SELECT
      p.date,
      p.ticker,
      p.adjusted_close AS close,
      p.volume,
      s.sector,
      f.pe_ratio,
      f.book_to_market
    FROM prices p
    JOIN securities s ON p.ticker = s.ticker
    LEFT JOIN fundamentals f
      ON p.ticker = f.ticker
      AND p.date = f.date
    WHERE p.date >= '2015-01-01'
    ORDER BY p.date, p.ticker
  """
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices
    volume: Numeric
    sector: String as sectors
    pe_ratio: Numeric
    book_to_market: Numeric
  options:
    sslmode = "require"
    statement_timeout = 120000

signal momentum:
  raw = zscore(ret(prices, 60))
  neutral = neutralize(raw, by=sectors)
  emit neutral

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• CSV Format - File-based loading
  • S3 Storage - Cloud storage
  • Data Quality - Validating your data

docs.title: S3 Storage
docs.text: Load data directly from Amazon S3 and compatible object stores.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://my-bucket/data/prices.parquet"
  format = parquet

docs.title: Environment Variables (Recommended)
docs.text:
Bash
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your_access_key
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your_secret_key
export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1

docs.title: AWS Profile
docs.text:
Bash
export AWS_PROFILE=my-profile

docs.title: IAM Role (EC2/ECS)
docs.text: When running on AWS infrastructure, IAM roles are automatically used.

docs.title: Explicit Credentials
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    aws_access_key_id = "${AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}"
    aws_secret_access_key = "${AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}"
    region = "us-east-1"

docs.title: Region
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    region = "us-west-2"

docs.title: Endpoint (S3-Compatible)
docs.text:
For MinIO, DigitalOcean Spaces, etc.:

Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    endpoint = "https://nyc3.digitaloceanspaces.com"
    region = "nyc3"

docs.title: Path Style
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    path_style = true  # Use path-style URLs (bucket in path)

docs.title: Glob Pattern
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data/*.parquet"
  format = parquet

docs.title: Prefix
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data/prices/"
  format = parquet
  options:
    recursive = true  # Include subdirectories

docs.title: Partitioned Data
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data/prices/"
  format = parquet
  options:
    partitioned = true
    start_date = "2023-01-01"

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
sigc caches S3 data locally for faster subsequent loads:

Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    cache = true                    # Enable caching (default)
    cache_dir = "/tmp/sigc_cache"   # Cache location
    cache_ttl = 3600                # TTL in seconds (1 hour)

docs.title: Disable Caching
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    cache = false  # Always fetch from S3

docs.title: Parallel Downloads
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data/*.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    max_connections = 8  # Parallel downloads

docs.title: Range Requests
docs.text:
Parquet files support reading only needed columns via range requests:

Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/large_file.parquet"
  format = parquet
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices
  # Only downloads the 'date', 'ticker', and 'close' columns

docs.title: Streaming
docs.text:
For very large files:

Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/huge_file.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    streaming = true  # Stream instead of download

docs.title: MinIO
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://my-bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    endpoint = "http://localhost:9000"
    path_style = true

docs.title: DigitalOcean Spaces
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://my-space/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    endpoint = "https://nyc3.digitaloceanspaces.com"
    region = "nyc3"

docs.title: Backblaze B2
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://my-bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    endpoint = "https://s3.us-west-002.backblazeb2.com"
    region = "us-west-002"

docs.title: Cloudflare R2
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://my-bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    endpoint = "https://account-id.r2.cloudflarestorage.com"

docs.title: Access Denied
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Access Denied to s3://bucket/file.parquet

Check:

  1. Credentials are set correctly
  2. Bucket policy allows access
  3. IAM permissions include s3:GetObject

docs.title: Bucket Not Found
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Bucket 'bucket-name' does not exist

Check:

  1. Bucket name is correct
  2. Region matches bucket region

docs.title: Timeout
docs.text:
Text Only
Error: Connection timeout

Solutions:

  1. Check network connectivity
  2. Increase timeout: options: timeout = 60
  3. Use VPC endpoint for AWS

docs.title: 1. Use Environment Variables
docs.text:
Bash
# .env or shell profile
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=xxx
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=xxx
export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1

docs.title: 2. Enable Caching for Development
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    cache = true
    cache_ttl = 86400  # 24 hours

docs.title: 3. Use Parquet Format
docs.text: Parquet's columnar format enables efficient partial reads from S3.

docs.title: 4. Partition Large Datasets
docs.text:
Text Only
s3://bucket/prices/
├── year=2023/
│   ├── month=01/
│   └── month=02/
└── year=2024/

docs.title: 5. Set Appropriate Timeouts
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://bucket/data.parquet"
  format = parquet
  options:
    timeout = 120      # 2 minutes
    max_retries = 3

docs.title: Example: Production Setup
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  source = "s3://prod-data/market/prices/"
  format = parquet
  options:
    partitioned = true
    region = "us-east-1"
    cache = true
    cache_dir = "/var/cache/sigc"
    cache_ttl = 3600
    max_connections = 4
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices
    volume: Numeric
    high: Numeric
    low: Numeric

signal momentum:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, 60))

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
1. Never hardcode credentials - Use environment variables or IAM roles
  2. Use least privilege - Grant only s3:GetObject on specific buckets
  3. Enable encryption - Use server-side encryption (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS)
  4. Use VPC endpoints - Keep traffic within AWS network
  5. Enable access logging - Audit who accesses your data

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL - Database integration
  • Parquet Format - Parquet file details
  • Data Quality - Validating your data

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to sigc! This section will help you get up and running quickly.

docs.title: Quick Start (5 minutes)
docs.text:
Already familiar with quant concepts? Jump straight in:

  1. Install sigc
  2. Run your first backtest

docs.title: Guided Tutorial (30 minutes)
docs.text:
New to sigc or want a thorough introduction:

  1. Install sigc
  2. Build your first strategy
  3. Set up your IDE

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
By the end of this section, you'll be able to:

  • Install the sigc binary on your system
  • Write a basic trading signal in the sigc DSL
  • Run a backtest and interpret the results
  • Set up VS Code for sigc development
  • Load and work with sample data

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • Operating System: Linux, macOS, or Windows (WSL2)
  • Rust Toolchain: Version 1.70 or later
  • Basic Knowledge: Familiarity with command-line tools

No Rust experience required

You don't need to know Rust to use sigc. The DSL is a separate language designed for quantitative research. Rust knowledge is only needed if you want to contribute to sigc itself or use the Rust API directly.

docs.title: Section Overview
docs.text: Page Description Time Installation Install Rust and build sigc 10 min Quickstart Run your first backtest 5 min First Strategy Build a complete momentum strategy 15 min IDE Setup Configure VS Code extension 5 min Sample Data Work with included datasets 5 min

docs.title: Essential Commands
docs.text:
Bash
# Compile a signal (validate syntax)
sigc compile strategy.sig

# Run a backtest
sigc run strategy.sig

# Export results to JSON
sigc run strategy.sig --output results.json

# Compare two strategies
sigc diff strategy_a.sig strategy_b.sig

docs.title: Basic Signal Structure
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"

params:
  lookback = 20

signal momentum:
  returns = ret(prices, lookback)
  emit zscore(returns)

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2024-01-01 to 2024-12-31

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Ready to begin? Start with Installation.

If you prefer learning by example, check out the Strategy Library for 23 complete, documented strategies.

docs.title: Your First Strategy
docs.text:
This tutorial walks you through building a complete momentum strategy from scratch. You'll learn the key concepts and best practices along the way.

Time Required

About 15-20 minutes to complete.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
We'll build a volatility-adjusted momentum strategy that:

  1. Computes 20-day returns for each asset
  2. Adjusts for volatility (so high-vol stocks don't dominate)
  3. Normalizes scores cross-sectionally
  4. Goes long top performers, short bottom performers
  5. Includes transaction cost modeling

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• sigc installed
  • Sample price data (we'll create it)

docs.title: Step 1: Set Up Your Project
docs.text:
Create a project directory:

Bash
mkdir momentum-strategy && cd momentum-strategy
mkdir data

docs.title: Create Price Data
docs.text:
Create data/prices.csv with at least 60 days of data for 10 stocks:

Bash
# Download sample data from the docs
curl -o data/prices.csv https://docs.skelfresearch.com/sigc/assets/sample-data/prices.csv

Or create your own CSV with columns: date,AAPL,MSFT,GOOGL,...

docs.title: Step 2: Basic Signal Structure
docs.text:
Create strategy.sig with the basic structure:

Text Only
// Volatility-Adjusted Momentum Strategy
// Author: Your Name
// Date: 2024-01-01

data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"

params:
  lookback = 20

signal momentum:
  returns = ret(prices, lookback)
  emit returns

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2024-01-02 to 2024-02-29

Test it:

Bash
sigc compile strategy.sig
sigc run strategy.sig

Understanding the Output

At this point, you should see backtest results. Don't worry if the Sharpe ratio is low - we'll improve it.

docs.title: Step 3: Add Volatility Adjustment
docs.text:
Raw momentum favors high-volatility stocks. Let's normalize by volatility:

Text Only
data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"

params:
  lookback = 20
  vol_lookback = 60

signal momentum:
  // Compute returns
  returns = ret(prices, lookback)

  // Compute volatility
  daily_ret = ret(prices, 1)
  volatility = rolling_std(daily_ret, vol_lookback)

  // Volatility-adjusted returns
  vol_adj = returns / volatility

  emit vol_adj

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2024-01-02 to 2024-02-29

Run again and compare:

Bash
sigc run strategy.sig

docs.title: Step 4: Cross-Sectional Normalization
docs.text:
Add z-score normalization to standardize scores:

Text Only
signal momentum:
  // Compute returns
  returns = ret(prices, lookback)

  // Compute volatility
  daily_ret = ret(prices, 1)
  volatility = rolling_std(daily_ret, vol_lookback)

  // Volatility-adjusted returns
  vol_adj = returns / volatility

  // Cross-sectional normalization
  normalized = zscore(vol_adj)

  emit normalized

The zscore function:

  1. Subtracts the cross-sectional mean
  2. Divides by the cross-sectional standard deviation
  3. Results in scores with mean=0, std=1

docs.title: Step 5: Handle Outliers
docs.text:
Extreme values can distort portfolio weights. Add winsorization:

Text Only
signal momentum:
  // Compute returns
  returns = ret(prices, lookback)

  // Compute volatility
  daily_ret = ret(prices, 1)
  volatility = rolling_std(daily_ret, vol_lookback)

  // Volatility-adjusted returns
  vol_adj = returns / volatility

  // Cross-sectional normalization
  normalized = zscore(vol_adj)

  // Clip outliers at 1st and 99th percentile
  cleaned = winsor(normalized, p=0.01)

  emit cleaned

docs.title: Step 6: Skip Recent Returns
docs.text:
Academic research shows that very recent returns tend to reverse (short-term reversal effect). Skip the most recent days:

Text Only
params:
  lookback = 20
  skip_days = 5
  vol_lookback = 60

signal momentum:
  // Total return over lookback period
  total_return = ret(prices, lookback)

  // Return in skip period (to subtract)
  recent_return = ret(prices, skip_days)

  // Momentum = total - recent (skip reversal)
  raw_momentum = total_return - recent_return

  // Volatility adjustment
  daily_ret = ret(prices, 1)
  volatility = rolling_std(daily_ret, vol_lookback)
  vol_adj = raw_momentum / volatility

  // Normalize and clean
  normalized = zscore(vol_adj)
  cleaned = winsor(normalized, p=0.01)

  emit cleaned

docs.title: Step 7: Add Position Limits
docs.text:
Limit individual position sizes to manage concentration risk:

Text Only
portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2, cap=0.1)
  backtest from 2024-01-02 to 2024-02-29

The cap=0.1 limits any single position to 10% of the portfolio.

docs.title: Complete Strategy
docs.text:
Here's the full strategy:

Text Only
// Volatility-Adjusted Momentum Strategy
// Computes 20-day returns, adjusts for volatility, skips recent days
// Goes long top 20%, short bottom 20% with 10% position cap

data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"

params:
  lookback = 20
  skip_days = 5
  vol_lookback = 60
  winsor_pct = 0.01
  top_pct = 0.2
  bottom_pct = 0.2
  max_weight = 0.1

signal momentum:
  // Total return over lookback period
  total_return = ret(prices, lookback)

  // Return in skip period (to subtract)
  recent_return = ret(prices, skip_days)

  // Momentum = total - recent (skip reversal)
  raw_momentum = total_return - recent_return

  // Volatility adjustment
  daily_ret = ret(prices, 1)
  volatility = rolling_std(daily_ret, vol_lookback)
  vol_adj = raw_momentum / volatility

  // Normalize and clean
  normalized = zscore(vol_adj)
  cleaned = winsor(normalized, winsor_pct)

  emit cleaned

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=top_pct, bottom=bottom_pct, cap=max_weight)
  backtest from 2024-01-02 to 2024-02-29

docs.title: Step 8: Run and Analyze
docs.text:
Bash
# Run backtest
sigc run strategy.sig

# Export detailed results
sigc run strategy.sig --output results.json

# View IR structure
sigc explain strategy.sig

docs.title: Interpret Results
docs.text:
Text Only
=== Backtest Results ===
Total Return:         12.45%
Annualized Return:    98.76%
Sharpe Ratio:          2.15
Max Drawdown:          4.32%
Turnover:            280.00%
Metric What It Means Good Range Sharpe Ratio Risk-adjusted return > 1.0 Max Drawdown Worst decline < 15% Turnover Trading frequency < 500%

docs.title: Step 9: Parameter Sensitivity
docs.text:
Test different parameter values:

Lookback = 10Lookback = 20Lookback = 40 Text Only
params:
  lookback = 10
  # ... rest same
Text Only
params:
  lookback = 20
  # ... rest same
Text Only
params:
  lookback = 40
  # ... rest same

Compare results:

Bash
sigc diff strategy_10d.sig strategy_20d.sig

docs.title: What You Learned
docs.text:
In this tutorial, you learned how to:

  • Structure a .sig file with data, params, signal, and portfolio blocks
  • Compute returns and volatility
  • Apply cross-sectional normalization with zscore
  • Handle outliers with winsor
  • Skip recent returns to avoid reversal
  • Set position limits with cap
  • Use parameters for easy tuning

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Comment your code - Explain the "why", not the "what"
  2. Use parameters - Make tunable values configurable
  3. Normalize signals - Use zscore for cross-sectional comparability
  4. Handle outliers - Use winsor to clip extremes
  5. Start simple - Add complexity gradually

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• IDE Setup - Get VS Code configured for sigc
  • DSL Basics - Deep dive into the language
  • Operators Reference - Explore all 120+ operators
  • Tutorials - More strategy tutorials
  • Strategy Library - Study complete strategies

docs.title: IDE Setup
docs.text: Set up VS Code for the best sigc development experience.

docs.title: VS Code Extension
docs.text:
The sigc VS Code extension provides:

  • Syntax highlighting for .sig files
  • 25+ code snippets for common patterns
  • Compile, Run, and Explain commands
  • Language Server Protocol (LSP) integration
  • Real-time error diagnostics
  • Hover documentation for operators
  • Code completion

docs.title: Step 1: Build the Extension
docs.text:
Bash
cd /path/to/sigc/editors/vscode

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Compile TypeScript
npm run compile

# Package as VSIX
npx @vscode/vsce package

This creates sigc-0.1.0.vsix.

docs.title: Step 2: Install in VS Code
docs.text:
1. Open VS Code
  2. Press Cmd+Shift+P (macOS) or Ctrl+Shift+P (Windows/Linux)
  3. Type "Install from VSIX"
  4. Select sigc-0.1.0.vsix
  5. Reload VS Code

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Installation
docs.text:
1. Create a new file with .sig extension
  2. You should see syntax highlighting
  3. Type signal and press Tab - a snippet should expand

docs.title: Syntax Highlighting
docs.text:
The extension highlights:

  • Keywords: data, params, signal, portfolio, fn, macro, emit, let
  • Operators: ret, zscore, rank, rolling_mean, etc.
  • Comments: // and # style
  • Strings and numbers

docs.title: Code Snippets
docs.text:
Type a prefix and press Tab to expand:

Prefix Expands To strategy Complete strategy template signal Signal block data Data section params Parameters section portfolio Portfolio block fn Function definition macro Macro definition momentum Momentum signal template meanrev Mean reversion template rmean rolling_mean(${1:x}, ${2:window}) rstd rolling_std(${1:x}, ${2:window}) zs zscore(${1:x}) win winsor(${1:x}, p=${2:0.01}) ret ret(${1:prices}, ${2:lookback}) lag lag(${1:x}, ${2:n}) ema ema(${1:x}, ${2:span}) rsi rsi(${1:x}, ${2:period}) macd macd(${1:x}, ${2:fast}, ${3:slow}, ${4:signal}) longshort long_short(top=${1:0.2}, bottom=${2:0.2}) where where(${1:cond}, ${2:true_val}, ${3:false_val})

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
Access via Command Palette (Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+P):

Command Description sigc: Compile Compile current file sigc: Run Run backtest sigc: Explain Show IR structure

You can also right-click in the editor for context menu options.

docs.title: Language Server (LSP)
docs.text: The LSP provides real-time feedback:

docs.title: Error Diagnostics
docs.text:
Errors appear as you type with red underlines:

Text Only
signal test:
  x = ret(prices, lookback)
  emit unknownfunction(x)  // Error: Unknown function 'unknownfunction'

docs.title: Hover Documentation
docs.text:
Hover over any operator to see its documentation:

Text Only
zscore(x)

Cross-sectional z-score normalization.
Subtracts mean and divides by standard deviation.

Input: Series (cross-sectional)
Output: Series with mean=0, std=1

Example:
  normalized = zscore(returns)

docs.title: Code Completion
docs.text:
Press Ctrl+Space for suggestions:

  • Built-in operators
  • Variables in scope
  • Parameters
  • Signals
  • Functions and macros

docs.title: Go to Definition
docs.text:
Cmd/Ctrl+Click or F12 on:

  • Signal references → jumps to signal definition
  • Function calls → jumps to function definition
  • Macro invocations → jumps to macro definition

docs.title: Document Outline
docs.text: View all signals, functions, and macros in the Outline panel (Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+O).

docs.title: Extension Settings
docs.text:
Open VS Code settings (Cmd+,) and search for "sigc":

Setting Default Description sigc.binaryPath sigc Path to sigc binary sigc.autoCompile true Compile on save sigc.showInlineHints true Show type hints

docs.title: Recommended VS Code Settings
docs.text:
Add to your settings.json:

JSON
{
  "[sig]": {
    "editor.tabSize": 2,
    "editor.insertSpaces": true,
    "editor.formatOnSave": false,
    "editor.wordWrap": "on"
  },
  "sigc.binaryPath": "/path/to/sigc/target/release/sigc",
  "sigc.autoCompile": true
}

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text:
Add custom keybindings in keybindings.json:

JSON
[
  {
    "key": "cmd+shift+r",
    "command": "sigc.run",
    "when": "editorLangId == sig"
  },
  {
    "key": "cmd+shift+c",
    "command": "sigc.compile",
    "when": "editorLangId == sig"
  }
]

docs.title: Extension Not Loading
docs.text:
1. Check VS Code's Output panel (View > Output) and select "sigc"
  2. Ensure the sigc binary is in your PATH or configured in settings

docs.title: No Syntax Highlighting
docs.text:
1. Verify the file has .sig extension
  2. Try reloading VS Code (Cmd+Shift+P → "Reload Window")
  3. Check that the extension is enabled

docs.title: LSP Not Working
docs.text:
The LSP requires sigc-lsp binary:

Bash
# Build the LSP
cd /path/to/sigc
cargo build --release -p sig_lsp

# Verify
./target/release/sig-lsp --version

Configure the path in settings:

JSON
{
  "sigc.lspPath": "/path/to/sigc/target/release/sig-lsp"
}

docs.title: Slow Diagnostics
docs.text:
For large files, try:

  1. Increase the diagnostic delay in settings
  2. Disable autoCompile and compile manually

docs.title: Vim/Neovim
docs.text:
Basic syntax highlighting:

VimL
" ~/.vim/syntax/sig.vim
syntax match sigKeyword /\<\(data\|params\|signal\|portfolio\|fn\|macro\|emit\|let\)\>/
syntax match sigOperator /\<\(ret\|lag\|zscore\|rank\|rolling_mean\|rolling_std\)\>/
syntax match sigComment /\/\/.*/
syntax match sigComment /#.*/

highlight link sigKeyword Keyword
highlight link sigOperator Function
highlight link sigComment Comment

docs.title: Sublime Text
docs.text: Create a .sublime-syntax file in your Packages/User directory.

docs.title: JetBrains IDEs
docs.text: No official plugin yet. Use the TextMate bundle for basic highlighting.

docs.text:

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide covers how to install sigc on your system.

docs.title: Rust Toolchain
docs.text:
sigc is written in Rust and requires the Rust toolchain to build from source.

Linux / macOSWindows Bash
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh

Follow the prompts and restart your terminal.

Download and run rustup-init.exe.

Windows Support

sigc is primarily developed on Linux/macOS. Windows users should use WSL2 for the best experience.

Verify your installation:

Bash
rustc --version
# rustc 1.70.0 or later

docs.title: Build from Source (Recommended)
docs.text:
Building from source ensures you have the latest features and optimizations for your system.

Bash
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/Skelf-Research/sigc.git
cd sigc

# Build in release mode
cargo build --release

# The binary is at ./target/release/sigc

Verify the build:

Bash
./target/release/sigc --version
# sigc 0.10.0

docs.title: Add to PATH
docs.text:
To use sigc from anywhere, add it to your PATH:

Linux / macOS (bash/zsh)Fish Bash
# Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc
export PATH="$PATH:/path/to/sigc/target/release"

# Reload shell
source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
Fish
# Add to ~/.config/fish/config.fish
set -gx PATH $PATH /path/to/sigc/target/release

docs.title: Pre-built Binaries
docs.text:
Pre-built binaries are available for common platforms on the GitHub Releases page.

Bash
# Download for Linux x86_64
curl -L https://github.com/Skelf-Research/sigc/releases/latest/download/sigc-linux-x86_64.tar.gz | tar xz

# Download for macOS (Apple Silicon)
curl -L https://github.com/Skelf-Research/sigc/releases/latest/download/sigc-darwin-arm64.tar.gz | tar xz

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Run these commands to verify sigc is installed correctly:

Bash
# Check version
sigc --version

# Show help
sigc --help

# Compile a test file
echo 'data:
  prices: load csv from "test.csv"
signal test:
  emit prices
portfolio main:
  weights = test
  backtest from 2024-01-01 to 2024-12-31' > test.sig

sigc compile test.sig

Expected output:

Text Only
INFO sigc: sigc v0.10.0
INFO sig_compiler: Parsing source
INFO sig_compiler: Parsed 1 data, 0 params, 1 signals, 1 portfolios
INFO sig_compiler: Lowered to 2 IR nodes
INFO sigc: Compilation complete: 2 nodes

docs.title: Optional: Python Integration
docs.text:
To use sigc from Python (Jupyter notebooks, scripts), install the pysigc package:

Bash
# From the sigc repository
cd crates/pysigc
pip install maturin
maturin develop --release

Verify Python installation:

Python
import pysigc
print(pysigc.__version__)

See Python Integration for detailed usage.

docs.title: Optional: VS Code Extension
docs.text:
For the best development experience, install the VS Code extension:

Bash
cd editors/vscode
npm install
npm run compile
npx @vscode/vsce package

Then install the generated sigc-0.1.0.vsix in VS Code.

See IDE Setup for detailed instructions.

docs.title: Build Fails with "linker not found"
docs.text:
Install build essentials:

Ubuntu/DebianFedoramacOS Bash
sudo apt install build-essential
Bash
sudo dnf install gcc
Bash
xcode-select --install

docs.title: Build Fails with OpenSSL Errors
docs.text:
Install OpenSSL development headers:

Ubuntu/DebianFedoramacOS Bash
sudo apt install libssl-dev pkg-config
Bash
sudo dnf install openssl-devel
Bash
brew install openssl
export OPENSSL_DIR=$(brew --prefix openssl)

docs.title: "Command not found: sigc"
docs.text:
Ensure the binary is in your PATH:

Bash
# Check if sigc is in PATH
which sigc

# If not found, add it
export PATH="$PATH:/path/to/sigc/target/release"

docs.title: Slow Build Times
docs.text:
Enable incremental compilation and use sccache:

Bash
# Install sccache
cargo install sccache

# Set as compiler wrapper
export RUSTC_WRAPPER=sccache

# Rebuild
cargo build --release

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that sigc is installed, continue to:

  • 5-Minute Quickstart - Run your first backtest
  • Your First Strategy - Build a complete strategy
  • IDE Setup - Configure VS Code

docs.title: 5-Minute Quickstart
docs.text:
Get your first signal running in 5 minutes.

Prerequisites

Make sure you've installed sigc before continuing.

docs.title: Step 2: Write Your First Signal
docs.text:
Create my_signal.sig:

Text Only
data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"

params:
  lookback = 5

signal momentum:
  returns = ret(prices, lookback)
  score = zscore(returns)
  emit score

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.4, bottom=0.4)
  backtest from 2024-01-02 to 2024-01-16

docs.title: What This Does
docs.text:
1. data: - Loads price data from a CSV file
  2. params: - Defines a tunable parameter (5-day lookback)
  3. signal momentum: - Computes 5-day returns and normalizes them
  4. portfolio main: - Longs top 40% performers, shorts bottom 40%
  5. backtest - Runs simulation from Jan 2 to Jan 16, 2024

docs.title: Step 3: Compile
docs.text:
Validate your signal compiles correctly:

Bash
sigc compile my_signal.sig

Expected output:

Text Only
INFO sigc: sigc v0.10.0
INFO sig_compiler: Parsing source
INFO sig_compiler: Parsed 1 data, 1 params, 1 signals, 1 portfolios
INFO sig_compiler: Lowered to 5 IR nodes
INFO sigc: Compilation complete: 5 nodes

Compilation passed

If you see "Compilation complete", your signal is syntactically correct and type-safe.

docs.title: Step 4: Run Backtest
docs.text:
Execute the backtest:

Bash
sigc run my_signal.sig

Expected output:

Text Only
=== Backtest Results ===
Total Return:          2.15%
Annualized Return:    78.45%
Sharpe Ratio:          3.21
Max Drawdown:          0.82%
Turnover:            180.00%

Metrics Explained

  • Total Return: Cumulative return over the backtest period
  • Annualized Return: Return scaled to a yearly basis
  • Sharpe Ratio: Risk-adjusted return (higher is better)
  • Max Drawdown: Largest peak-to-trough decline
  • Turnover: How much the portfolio changes (annualized)

docs.title: Step 5: Export Results
docs.text:
Save results to JSON for further analysis:

Bash
sigc run my_signal.sig --output results.json

The JSON file contains:

JSON
{
  "metrics": {
    "total_return": 0.0215,
    "annualized_return": 0.7845,
    "sharpe_ratio": 3.21,
    "max_drawdown": 0.0082,
    "turnover": 1.80
  },
  "returns_series": [...],
  "positions": {...}
}

docs.title: What Just Happened?
docs.text:
Here's the execution flow:

graph LR
    A[Load CSV] --> B[Compute Returns]
    B --> C[Z-Score Normalize]
    C --> D[Rank Assets]
    D --> E[Assign Weights]
    E --> F[Simulate Trading]
    F --> G[Calculate Metrics]
  1. Data loaded: Prices were read from CSV into a columnar DataFrame
  2. Signal computed: For each asset, computed 5-day returns then z-scored cross-sectionally
  3. Weights assigned: Ranked assets, assigned +weight to top 40%, -weight to bottom 40%
  4. Backtest run: Simulated daily rebalancing
  5. Metrics calculated: Sharpe, drawdown, turnover computed from daily returns

docs.title: Change the Lookback Period
docs.text:
Edit my_signal.sig:

Text Only
params:
  lookback = 10  # Changed from 5 to 10

Run again and compare results:

Bash
sigc run my_signal.sig

docs.title: Add Winsorization
docs.text:
Clean outliers by adding winsor:

Text Only
signal momentum:
  returns = ret(prices, lookback)
  score = zscore(returns)
  cleaned = winsor(score, p=0.05)  # Clip at 5th/95th percentile
  emit cleaned

docs.title: Compare Strategies
docs.text:
Save the original, modify, and compare:

Bash
# Rename original
mv my_signal.sig momentum_5d.sig

# Create new version with lookback=10
# ... edit file ...

# Compare
sigc diff momentum_5d.sig momentum_10d.sig

docs.title: "Failed to load CSV"
docs.text:
Check the file path:

Bash
ls -la data/prices.csv

Ensure the path in your .sig file matches.

docs.title: "No data sources found"
docs.text:
Verify your data: block format:

Text Only
data:
  prices: load csv from "path/to/file.csv"

docs.title: Low Sharpe Ratio
docs.text:
With limited data, results may be noisy. Try:

  • Longer backtest period
  • More assets in universe
  • Different lookback parameters

docs.text:

  • Operators Reference - Explore 120+ built-in operators

docs.title: Sample Data
docs.text: This guide covers working with sample data for sigc development and testing.

docs.title: Included Sample Data
docs.text: The documentation includes sample price data you can use for testing:

docs.title: Download Sample Data
docs.text:
Bash
# From the docs assets
curl -o data/prices.csv https://docs.skelfresearch.com/sigc/assets/sample-data/prices.csv

Or copy from the repository:

Bash
cp /path/to/sigc/documentation/docs/assets/sample-data/prices.csv data/

docs.title: Sample Data Format
docs.text:
The included prices.csv contains:

  • Period: January - February 2024 (40 trading days)
  • Assets: 10 stocks (AAPL, MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN, META, NVDA, TSLA, JPM, V, JNJ)
  • Fields: Date and adjusted close prices
Text Only
date,AAPL,MSFT,GOOGL,AMZN,META,NVDA,TSLA,JPM,V,JNJ
2024-01-02,185.64,374.58,140.25,151.94,346.29,481.68,248.42,170.10,260.35,156.74
2024-01-03,184.25,373.31,139.12,149.93,344.47,477.80,246.98,169.62,258.87,155.89
...

docs.title: CSV Files
docs.text:
sigc expects CSV files with:

  • First row: Column headers
  • First column: Date (YYYY-MM-DD format)
  • Remaining columns: Asset prices
Text Only
date,ASSET1,ASSET2,ASSET3
2024-01-02,100.00,200.00,300.00
2024-01-03,101.00,199.00,302.00

docs.title: Supported Date Formats
docs.text:
• YYYY-MM-DD (preferred): 2024-01-15
  • YYYY/MM/DD: 2024/01/15
  • MM/DD/YYYY: 01/15/2024

docs.title: Missing Data
docs.text:
sigc handles missing data (NaN) automatically. Use empty cells or literal "NaN":

Text Only
date,AAPL,MSFT
2024-01-02,185.64,374.58
2024-01-03,,373.31
2024-01-04,181.91,

Use fill_nan to handle missing values in your signals:

Text Only
signal cleaned:
  filled = fill_nan(prices, 0)
  emit filled

docs.title: From Yahoo Finance
docs.text:
Using Python:

Python
import yfinance as yf
import pandas as pd

# Download data
tickers = ['AAPL', 'MSFT', 'GOOGL', 'AMZN', 'META']
data = yf.download(tickers, start='2020-01-01', end='2024-01-01')

# Extract adjusted close prices
prices = data['Adj Close']

# Save to CSV
prices.to_csv('data/prices.csv')

docs.title: From Alpaca
docs.text:
Python
from alpaca.data import StockHistoricalDataClient
from alpaca.data.requests import StockBarsRequest
from alpaca.data.timeframe import TimeFrame

client = StockHistoricalDataClient('api_key', 'secret_key')

request = StockBarsRequest(
    symbol_or_symbols=['AAPL', 'MSFT', 'GOOGL'],
    timeframe=TimeFrame.Day,
    start='2020-01-01',
    end='2024-01-01'
)

bars = client.get_stock_bars(request)
df = bars.df.unstack(level=0)['close']
df.to_csv('data/prices.csv')

docs.title: Generate Synthetic Data
docs.text:
For testing, generate random price data:

Python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)

dates = pd.date_range('2024-01-01', periods=252, freq='B')
assets = ['STOCK_A', 'STOCK_B', 'STOCK_C', 'STOCK_D', 'STOCK_E']

# Generate random returns
returns = np.random.normal(0.0005, 0.02, (252, 5))

# Convert to prices
prices = 100 * np.cumprod(1 + returns, axis=0)

df = pd.DataFrame(prices, index=dates, columns=assets)
df.index.name = 'date'
df.to_csv('data/synthetic_prices.csv')

docs.title: Free Data Sources
docs.text: Source Data Type Frequency Notes Yahoo Finance Prices, volumes Daily Free, reliable FRED Economic data Various US economic indicators Quandl (free tier) Various Daily Limited free access Alpha Vantage Prices Daily API key required

docs.title: Commercial Data Sources
docs.text: Source Data Type Notes Bloomberg Everything Industry standard Refinitiv Prices, fundamentals Good corporate data IEX Cloud US equities Developer-friendly Polygon.io US equities Real-time available Alpaca US equities Free with brokerage

docs.title: CSV Files
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"
  volume: load csv from "data/volume.csv"

docs.title: Parquet Files
docs.text:
Parquet is more efficient for large datasets:

Text Only
data:
  prices: load parquet from "data/prices.parquet"

Convert CSV to Parquet using Python:

Python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('data/prices.csv', index_col=0, parse_dates=True)
df.to_parquet('data/prices.parquet')

docs.title: S3 Data
docs.text:
Load directly from AWS S3:

Text Only
data:
  prices: load parquet from "s3://my-bucket/prices.parquet"

Set credentials via environment variables:

Bash
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your_key
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your_secret

docs.title: Multiple Data Sources
docs.text:
Combine multiple data files:

Text Only
data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"
  volume: load csv from "data/volume.csv"
  market_cap: load parquet from "data/fundamentals.parquet"
  sectors: load csv from "data/sectors.csv"

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before using data, check for issues:

Bash
# Quick Python check
python -c "
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('data/prices.csv', index_col=0, parse_dates=True)
print(f'Shape: {df.shape}')
print(f'Date range: {df.index[0]} to {df.index[-1]}')
print(f'Missing values:\n{df.isna().sum()}')
print(f'Sample:\n{df.head()}')
"

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text: Issue Solution Missing dates Fill forward or interpolate Missing values Use fill_nan or coalesce Outliers Use winsor to clip extremes Stale data Check data freshness before backtesting Survivorship bias Include delisted securities

docs.title: Corporate Actions
docs.text:
For accurate backtesting, adjust for:

  • Splits: Divide historical prices by split ratio
  • Dividends: Use adjusted close prices
  • Mergers: Handle symbol changes

Yahoo Finance's "Adj Close" already handles splits and dividends.

docs.title: Example: Multi-Asset Strategy
docs.text:
Using the sample data:

Text Only
// Multi-asset momentum strategy using sample data

data:
  prices: load csv from "data/prices.csv"

params:
  lookback = 20
  vol_window = 60

signal momentum:
  // Compute returns
  returns = ret(prices, lookback)

  // Volatility adjustment
  vol = rolling_std(ret(prices, 1), vol_window)
  vol_adj = returns / vol

  // Normalize
  emit zscore(vol_adj)

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2024-01-16 to 2024-02-29

sigc integrates with:

Category Integrations Languages Python IDEs VSCode Brokers Alpaca Data Yahoo Finance Streaming Real-time data

import pysigc

docs.title: Data Provider
Execution Provider
1. Use Paper Trading First
docs.text:
YAML
output:
  type: alpaca
  alpaca:
    paper: true  # Always start with paper

docs.title: 2. Handle Rate Limits
docs.text:
YAML
data:
  provider: yahoo
  rate_limit:
    requests_per_minute: 30

docs.title: 3. Cache External Data
docs.text:
YAML
data:
  cache:
    enabled: true
    ttl_hours: 24

docs.title: 4. Error Handling
docs.text:
Python
try:
    results = pysigc.run("strategy.sig")
except pysigc.DataError as e:
    print(f"Data issue: {e}")
except pysigc.ExecutionError as e:
    print(f"Execution issue: {e}")

docs.title: Alpaca Integration
docs.text: Execute trades through Alpaca Markets.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Alpaca provides:

  • Commission-free trading
  • Paper trading for testing
  • REST and WebSocket APIs
  • Extended hours trading

docs.title: Create Account
docs.text:
1. Sign up at alpaca.markets
  2. Generate API keys in dashboard
  3. Note your API key and secret

docs.title: Configure sigc
docs.text:
YAML
# sigc.yaml
output:
  type: alpaca

  alpaca:
    api_key: ${ALPACA_API_KEY}
    api_secret: ${ALPACA_API_SECRET}
    base_url: https://api.alpaca.markets  # Live
    # base_url: https://paper-api.alpaca.markets  # Paper
    paper: true  # Start with paper trading

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Bash
export ALPACA_API_KEY=your_api_key
export ALPACA_API_SECRET=your_api_secret

docs.title: Paper Trading
docs.text:
Always test with paper trading first:

YAML
output:
  type: alpaca
  alpaca:
    paper: true  # Paper trading mode

Paper trading:

  • Uses separate paper account
  • Same API, different endpoint
  • Real market data
  • Simulated execution

docs.title: Market Orders
docs.text:
YAML
output:
  alpaca:
    orders:
      type: market
      time_in_force: day

docs.title: Limit Orders
docs.text:
YAML
output:
  alpaca:
    orders:
      type: limit
      limit_offset_pct: 0.1  # 0.1% better than market
      time_in_force: day

docs.title: Order Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Values Description type market, limit, stop, stop_limit Order type time_in_force day, gtc, ioc, fok Time in force extended_hours true, false Trade in extended hours

docs.title: Basic Execution
docs.text:
YAML
output:
  alpaca:
    execution:
      algorithm: immediate  # Submit all orders at once

docs.title: TWAP Execution
docs.text:
YAML
output:
  alpaca:
    execution:
      algorithm: twap
      duration_minutes: 30
      slices: 6  # 5-minute intervals

docs.title: VWAP Execution
docs.text:
YAML
output:
  alpaca:
    execution:
      algorithm: vwap
      duration_minutes: 60
      participation_rate: 0.05  # 5% of volume

docs.title: Smart Routing
docs.text:
YAML
output:
  alpaca:
    execution:
      smart_routing: true
      min_quantity: 100
      round_lots: true

docs.title: Sync Mode
docs.text:
YAML
output:
  alpaca:
    sync:
      mode: full  # full | incremental
      reconcile: true

docs.title: Full Sync
docs.text:
Closes all positions not in target weights:

YAML
sync:
  mode: full
  close_unlisted: true  # Close positions not in signal

docs.title: Incremental Sync
docs.text:
Only adjusts positions in target:

YAML
sync:
  mode: incremental
  close_unlisted: false

docs.title: Configure Multiple Accounts
Example: Full Configuration
docs.text:
YAML
# sigc.yaml
output:
  type: alpaca

  alpaca:
    api_key: ${ALPACA_API_KEY}
    api_secret: ${ALPACA_API_SECRET}
    paper: false  # Live trading

    orders:
      type: limit
      limit_offset_pct: 0.05
      time_in_force: day
      extended_hours: false

    execution:
      algorithm: twap
      duration_minutes: 30

    sync:
      mode: full
      reconcile: true
      close_unlisted: true

    safety:
      max_position_pct: 0.05
      max_order_value: 100000
      check_buying_power: true

    retry:
      max_attempts: 3
      delay_seconds: 5

    streaming:
      enabled: true
      events: [trade_updates]

Python
import pysigc

# Run a strategy file

# Access results

strategy = """
data:
  source = "prices.csv"
  format = csv
  columns:
    date: Date
    ticker: Symbol
    close: Numeric as prices

signal momentum:
  emit zscore(ret(prices, 60))

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(momentum).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
  backtest from 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31
"""

results = pysigc.run_string(strategy)

docs.title: Grid Search
docs.text:
Python
import pysigc
import pandas as pd

# Define parameter grid
lookbacks = [20, 40, 60, 80, 100]
top_pcts = [0.1, 0.2, 0.3]

results_list = []

for lookback in lookbacks:
    for top_pct in top_pcts:
        result = pysigc.run(
            "strategy.sig",
            params={"lookback": lookback, "top_pct": top_pct}
        )
        results_list.append({
            "lookback": lookback,
            "top_pct": top_pct,
            "sharpe": result.sharpe_ratio,
            "return": result.total_return,
            "drawdown": result.max_drawdown
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(results_list)
print(df.sort_values("sharpe", ascending=False))

docs.title: Parallel Optimization
docs.text:
Python
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
import pysigc

def run_with_params(params):
    return pysigc.run("strategy.sig", params=params)

param_sets = [
    {"lookback": 20, "top_pct": 0.2},
    {"lookback": 40, "top_pct": 0.2},
    {"lookback": 60, "top_pct": 0.2},
    # ...
]

with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as executor:
    results = list(executor.map(run_with_params, param_sets))

docs.title: Real-Time Data
docs.text:
Python
import pysigc

# Connect to live data
client = pysigc.LiveClient()

# Subscribe to updates
@client.on_bar
def on_new_bar(bar):
    # Recompute signals
    signals = pysigc.compute_signal("momentum", data=bar)
    print(f"New signal: {signals}")

client.subscribe(["AAPL", "MSFT", "GOOGL"])
client.run()

docs.title: Feature Engineering
docs.text:
Python
import pysigc
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# Get signal values as features
results = pysigc.run("feature_signals.sig")
signals = results.signals

# Combine signals into feature matrix
features = pd.merge(
    signals["momentum"],
    signals["value"],
    on=["date", "ticker"],
    suffixes=["_mom", "_val"]
)

# Add target (future returns)
features["target"] = features.groupby("ticker")["close"].pct_change(5).shift(-5) > 0

# Train model
X = features[["momentum", "value"]]
y = features["target"]
model = RandomForestClassifier().fit(X, y)

docs.title: Using Predictions
docs.text:
Python
# Generate predictions
features["ml_signal"] = model.predict_proba(X)[:, 1]

# Use in sigc strategy
strategy = """
signal ml_alpha:
  emit ml_predictions

portfolio main:
  weights = rank(ml_alpha).long_short(top=0.2, bottom=0.2)
"""

results = pysigc.run_string(
    strategy,
    data={"ml_predictions": features[["date", "ticker", "ml_signal"]]}
)

pysigc.run(

docs.title: pysigc.run_string()
docs.text:

pysigc.run_string(

    config: dict = None
) -> Results

# Just compile (no run)
compiled = pysigc.compile("strategy.sig")
print(compiled.signals)  # List of signals
print(compiled.portfolios)  # List of portfolios

# Compile once

# Run multiple times with different params

    results = compiled.run(params=params)

docs.title: Streaming Data
docs.text: Real-time and streaming data integration.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
sigc supports streaming data for:

  • Real-time signal updates
  • Intraday strategies
  • Live monitoring
  • Production trading

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text: Provider Data Types Latency Polygon Trades, quotes, bars ~50ms Alpaca Trades, quotes, bars ~100ms IEX Cloud Trades, quotes ~50ms Custom Any Varies

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
YAML
data:
  type: streaming
  provider: polygon

  polygon:
    api_key: ${POLYGON_API_KEY}
    feed: delayed  # delayed | real-time

docs.title: Subscribe to Symbols
docs.text:
YAML
data:
  type: streaming
  provider: polygon

  polygon:
    api_key: ${POLYGON_API_KEY}

  subscriptions:
    - type: bars
      symbols: ["AAPL", "MSFT", "GOOGL"]
      timeframe: 1min

    - type: trades
      symbols: ["AAPL"]

    - type: quotes
      symbols: ["AAPL"]

docs.title: Data Types
docs.text: Type Description Fields bars OHLCV candles open, high, low, close, volume trades Individual trades price, size, timestamp quotes Bid/ask quotes bid, ask, bid_size, ask_size

docs.text:
YAML

  type: streaming
  provider: alpaca

  alpaca:
    api_key: ${ALPACA_API_KEY}
    api_secret: ${ALPACA_API_SECRET}
    feed: iex  # iex | sip

docs.title: Subscriptions

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
YAML
data:
  type: streaming
  provider: websocket

  websocket:
    url: wss://your-data-source.com/stream
    auth:
      type: bearer
      token: ${WS_TOKEN}

    subscribe_message: |
      {"action": "subscribe", "symbols": ${symbols}}

    parser:
      type: json
      timestamp_field: "t"
      symbol_field: "s"
      price_field: "p"
      volume_field: "v"

docs.title: Real-Time Signal
docs.text:
Text Only
data:
  type: streaming
  provider: polygon
  subscriptions:
    - type: bars
      symbols: ["AAPL", "MSFT", "GOOGL"]
      timeframe: 1min

signal intraday_momentum:
  // Rolling calculations on streaming data
  ret_5m = ret(close, 5)
  ret_15m = ret(close, 15)

  momentum = ret_5m - ret_15m

  emit zscore(momentum)

portfolio live:
  weights = rank(intraday_momentum).long_short(top=0.3, bottom=0.3)

  // Real-time execution
  execute:
    on_signal_change: true
    min_change: 0.05  # Minimum 5% weight change

docs.title: Event-Driven Updates
docs.text:
YAML
streaming:
  update_policy:
    type: on_bar        # on_bar | on_trade | interval
    # type: interval
    # interval_seconds: 60

  buffer:
    type: rolling
    window: 100         # Keep last 100 bars

  warm_up:
    bars: 20            # Require 20 bars before signals

docs.title: Rolling Buffer
docs.text:
YAML
streaming:
  buffer:
    type: rolling
    window: 1000        # Keep last 1000 bars
    persist: true       # Save to disk

docs.title: Time Window
docs.text:
YAML
streaming:
  buffer:
    type: time
    window: 1h          # Keep last 1 hour

docs.title: Memory Management
docs.text:
YAML

  memory:
    max_buffer_mb: 500
    gc_interval_seconds: 60

docs.title: Bar Aggregation
docs.text:

    from: 1min
    to: 5min
    method: ohlcv       # Combine into OHLCV

docs.title: Custom Aggregation

  aggregation:
    from: trades
    to: bars
    interval: 1min
    fields:
      open: first
      high: max
      low: min
      close: last
      volume: sum

docs.title: Update Frequency
docs.text:
YAML

  signals:
    update_frequency: on_bar  # on_bar | interval | on_change
    # update_interval_seconds: 30

docs.title: Throttling
docs.text:
YAML

  signals:
    throttle:
      max_updates_per_minute: 10

docs.title: Real-Time Execution
docs.text:
YAML
streaming:
  execution:
    enabled: true
    provider: alpaca

    triggers:
      - type: signal_change
        threshold: 0.05   # 5% weight change
        action: rebalance

      - type: scheduled
        cron: "0 */30 9-15 * * 1-5"  # Every 30 min during market
        action: rebalance

docs.title: Order Staging
docs.text:
YAML

  execution:
    staging:
      enabled: true
      review_seconds: 60  # Manual review period
      auto_approve: false

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text:
Text Only
sigc_streaming_messages_total{provider="polygon",type="bars"} 15234
sigc_streaming_latency_ms{provider="polygon"} 45
sigc_streaming_errors_total{provider="polygon"} 3
sigc_streaming_reconnects_total{provider="polygon"} 2

docs.title: Health Checks
docs.text:
YAML
streaming:
  health:
    heartbeat_interval_seconds: 30
    max_silence_seconds: 60
    on_disconnect: reconnect  # reconnect | alert | halt

docs.title: Reconnection
docs.text:
YAML
streaming:
  reconnect:
    enabled: true
    max_attempts: 10
    initial_delay_seconds: 1
    max_delay_seconds: 60
    backoff: exponential

docs.title: Data Gaps
docs.text:
YAML
streaming:
  gaps:
    detection: true
    max_gap_seconds: 120
    on_gap: backfill  # backfill | interpolate | alert

docs.title: Stale Data
docs.text:
YAML
streaming:
  stale_data:
    threshold_seconds: 300
    action: alert  # alert | halt | use_last

docs.title: Example: Complete Streaming Setup
docs.text:
YAML
# sigc.yaml
data:
  type: streaming
  provider: polygon

  polygon:
    api_key: ${POLYGON_API_KEY}
    feed: delayed

  subscriptions:
    - type: bars
      symbols: ["AAPL", "MSFT", "GOOGL", "AMZN", "META",
                "NVDA", "TSLA", "JPM", "JNJ", "V"]
      timeframe: 1min

streaming:
  buffer:
    type: rolling
    window: 500
    persist: true

  update_policy:
    type: on_bar

  warm_up:
    bars: 60

  signals:
    update_frequency: on_bar
    throttle:
      max_updates_per_minute: 6

  execution:
    enabled: true
    provider: alpaca
    triggers:
      - type: signal_change
        threshold: 0.05
        action: rebalance

  reconnect:
    enabled: true
    max_attempts: 10

  health:
    heartbeat_interval_seconds: 30
    max_silence_seconds: 120

output:
  type: alpaca
  alpaca:
    paper: true
    orders:
      type: limit

docs.title: VSCode Extension
docs.text: Full IDE support for sigc development.

docs.title: From Marketplace

1. Open VSCode
  2. Go to Extensions (Ctrl+Shift+X)
  3. Search for "sigc"
  4. Click Install

docs.title: From Command Line
docs.text:
Bash
code --install-extension skelf-research.sigc-vscode

docs.title: Syntax Highlighting
docs.text:
Full syntax highlighting for .sig files:

  • Keywords (data, signal, portfolio, emit)
  • Operators (zscore, rank, rolling_mean)
  • Numbers, strings, comments
  • Type annotations

docs.title: Code Completion
docs.text:
IntelliSense for:

  • Operators: All 120+ operators with signatures
  • Keywords: Language keywords
  • Parameters: Function parameters
  • Variables: Defined variables in scope

Press Ctrl+Space to trigger completion.

docs.title: Hover Documentation
docs.text:
Hover over any operator to see:

  • Function signature
  • Description
  • Parameters
  • Return type
  • Example usage

docs.title: Error Diagnostics
docs.text:
Real-time error checking:

  • Syntax errors
  • Type mismatches
  • Undefined variables
  • Invalid operator usage

Errors appear with red underlines and in the Problems panel.

docs.title: Go to Definition

Ctrl+Click or F12 to jump to:

  • Signal definitions

  • Function definitions

docs.title: Find References

Shift+F12 to find all usages of:

  • Signals
  • Variables
  • Functions

Shift+Alt+F to format your strategy:

  • Consistent indentation
  • Proper spacing
  • Aligned operators

docs.title: Code Folding
docs.text:
Collapse sections:

  • data: blocks
  • signal blocks
  • portfolio blocks

docs.title: Extension Settings
docs.text:
Open Settings (Ctrl+,) and search for "sigc":

JSON
{
  // Path to sigc binary
  "sigc.binaryPath": "/usr/local/bin/sigc",

  // Enable real-time diagnostics
  "sigc.enableDiagnostics": true,

  // Diagnostic delay (ms)
  "sigc.diagnosticDelay": 500,

  // Format on save
  "sigc.formatOnSave": true,

  // Show inline hints
  "sigc.inlayHints.enabled": true,

  // LSP log level
  "sigc.logLevel": "info"
}

docs.title: Workspace Settings
